Sounds like @Ciberth is using the [Windows Subsystem for Linux](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_Subsystem_for_Linux) (WSL). Here's Microsoft's About page for it:

https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/commandline/wsl/about

**TL;DR:** The Windows NT kernel isn't actually tied to the Win32 API. It was explicitly developed with the intent of supporting the Win32, OS/2, and POSIX APIs as equal siblings via "subsystems" (that's also why NTFS has a POSIX mode which allows characters like `:` in filenames).

Over the course of the NT kernel's lifetime, Microsoft paired the POSIX-side support with various userlands (eg. Interix) and called it [Windows Services for UNIX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_Services_for_UNIX). That got deprecated in Windows 8 and removed in Windows 8.1 and WSL is the effective successor.

The augmenters in that example are connected with `iaa.Sometimes(p, augmenter)`, which means that each augmenter is only applied to `p` percent of all images. If every augmenter was applied to every image, the images would end up being completely broken. This also improves performance and makes sure that the model learns to understand examples without specific augmentation techniques.

I think you have the same misunderstanding as in issue #112. After using `to_deterministic()`, the output of `augment_images()` is the same if the input is the same AND the position in the batch is the same. So if you provide a batch of three images to `augment_images()`, the first image will be augmented using parameters A, the second using parameters B and the third using parameters C. When you call the function again, the sampled parameters will be again A, B and C (without determinism it would then be D, E, F). A, B and C can be different from each other and in your case usually will be.

> if the kernel gets locked up or some task runs away forever, the monitor can still operate and (potentially) show some useful

I think running it in a thread pool executor forever until the kernel shuts down should have the same effect, shouldn't it?

Other than that, I cat't imagine how to properly terminate the monitor if it's being run in a thread executor, so probably this is not so good idea :)

Been thinking about this a bit more.  Are you thinking that their ought to be a special Shutdown exception one could raise in a coroutine and have that shut down the entire kernel and all running tasks?

> - So for Curio the moral of the story is
> 
> await revc/read()
> await recv/read(1000)
> await send/write()
> 
> should not do anything more than blocking
> counterparts. And they should return ONLY AFTER
> REALLY COMPLETING the operation.

The current version of Curio already provides these semantics.   

How does a recv() call work with threads?  If data is available, it is returned immediately.  If not, the calling thread is blocked by the OS kernel and a new thread is scheduled.

How does a recv() call with with Curio?  If data is available, it is returned immediately. If not, the calling task is blocked by the Curio kernel and a new task is scheduled.

The only difference between the two concerns who is responsible for blocking the caller when no data is available.  Otherwise, the semantics are the same--as currently implemented in Curio.    I don't get this "ONLY AFTER REALLY COMPLETING" business.   If curio tries a recv() call and it runs without an exception, then the operation has, in fact, completed.   It's not an "optimization."  It's not a "hack." There is nothing to see here.  

The issue at hand in this report is whether or not Curio should *additionally* invoke the scheduler in all I/O operations.   I don't think it should.  And neither should anyone else who wants the semantics to be exactly the same as blocking I/O in synchronous code.

>> It does not save the graph of dependencies though.
>Yes, the FoBiS.py constructed dependency is into the log (that is created if you pass --log) not into the >fobos, but it is a choice: I hate to write makefile for big project and one thing boring is exactly the >creation of the compilation hierarchy rules.

Sure, this is always this balance about control (takes time, cumbersome) and automation (easy, but no control in case of mistake). I was simply suggesting to make it possible for the user to have more _control_, in case something goes wrong - and at least, as a psychological tranquilizer when adopting a new tool (that latter reason also contributing to me insisting on generating a reliable Makefile from FoBiS.py). I keep thinking that a more deterministic mode (providing the exclusive list of files entering the project) would not be a bad option (or alternatively, re-read the log file - not yet checked what is inside).

My other suggestion of an optional `directories=` parameter goes in the same direction (enhance user's _control_). I mean simply, indicate the directories to be searched _non-recursively_ for source files (note that most unix command do not attempt recursive search - probably because it is a dangerous feature - unless you explicitly ask for it with -r). I do not know whether you ever used python's distutils and its setup.py file, but there if you actually install a package (several files under a directory) instead of just a module (a single file.py), you are required to indicate which folders to consider as part of the package. So taking your "cumbersone" example from the README, the option would be:

    directories = ["src/", "src/nested-1", "src/nested-1/nested-2"] 

The number of folders do not scale up as quickly as the number of files, and such a list is already quite _informative_ when just having a look at it, regarding what is in the project, where the source files are etc...Besides, you can easily copy a folder from src_proj1 to src_proj1.old and keep editing src_proj1, without triggering an error, and without having to change your build script with a --exclude flag.

The code's explicitly returning `frozenset`s:

```python
def get_metadata(feature_name, etextno):
    """Looks up the value of a meta-data feature for a given text.
    Arguments:
        feature_name (str): The name of the meta-data to look up.
        etextno (int): The identifier of the Gutenberg text for which to look
            up the meta-data.
    Returns:
        frozenset: The values of the meta-data for the text or an empty set if
            the text does not have meta-data associated with the feature.
    Raises:
        NotImplementedError: If there is no MetadataExtractor registered that
            can extract meta-data for the given feature name.
    """
    metadata_values = MetadataExtractor.get(feature_name).get_metadata(etextno)
    return frozenset(metadata_values)


def get_etexts(feature_name, value):
    """Looks up all the texts that have meta-data matching some criterion.
    Arguments:
        feature_name (str): The the meta-data on which to select the texts.
        value (str): The value of the meta-data on which to filter the texts.
    Returns:
        frozenset: The set of all the Project Gutenberg text identifiers that
            match the provided query.
    Raises:
        NotImplementedError: If there is no MetadataExtractor registered that
            can extract meta-data for the given feature name.
    """
    matching_etexts = MetadataExtractor.get(feature_name).get_etexts(value)
    return frozenset(matching_etexts)

Ok, I gave a thought one more time. Options are these:
1. Decouple `Alignat` from `Math`. Then there are several ways to achieve the functionality I have implemented.
2. Leave it as it is: the main advantage is that there will be no code duplication for keyword args in `Math` - whatever you implement in `Math` is readily available in `Alignat` via `add_math` method.

I prefer 2. Thus raw argument is useful - this will allow Math to be used by other math environments if implemented in the future via the same way. Furthermore, if user wants to define his/her own environment on the spot and use `Math` functionality, then all what needs to be done is `raw=True`. I think this allows healthy flexibility.

We need the throw (std::exception) because destructors are noexcept by default in C++11 (see http://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/language/noexcept_spec). Without it, if any of those tests fail in test4_c2 the program will be abruptly terminated.

So the right thing to do is to disable this visual studio warning in just this test file with a #pragma.

For those unaware of kwallet behavior, it allows to store passwords in a hierarchical structure. Today, python-keyring stores every passwords it manages into a dedicated "Python" folder, which is solely used by python-keyring.

jnelson > it seems kwallet proposes by default some folders which look reasonable to use, like the "Passwords" folder. Would it make sense to store things inside this one? Or should we rename "Python" into "python-keyring" so that it's clearer?

The find_prompt() method is inherently finding only the last line there i.e. `A:admin@pe1#`. I don't think I would want multiline prompt in Netmiko as it would be a departure from how it is done everywhere else (and I somehwat doubt it is necessary).

Note, pattern by default will use the trailing prompt to determine whether Netmiko is done reading.

```
    def check_config_mode(self, check_string='', pattern=''):
        """Checks if the device is in configuration mode or not."""
        log.debug("pattern: {0}".format(pattern))
        self.write_channel('\n')
        output = self.read_until_pattern(pattern=pattern)
        log.debug("check_config_mode: {0}".format(repr(output)))
        return check_string in output
```

So I think you could do something like the following:

```
    def check_config_mode(self, check_string='configure'):
        """Checks if the device is in configuration mode or not."""
        return super(YourClass, self).check_config_mode(check_string=check_string)
```

In other words you know you are in configuration mode if the 'configure' string is returned when you hit enter.

Just to be clear... when you say multiple connections you mean using multiple Peripheral objects to connect to the same bluetooth device?

The relevant Bluetooth documentation page is this: https://developer.bluetooth.org/gatt/descriptors/Pages/DescriptorViewer.aspx?u=org.bluetooth.descriptor.gatt.client_characteristic_configuration.xml

You need to find the handle for the Client Characteristic Configuration register and write a 2 byte value to it (01 00 to enable, 00 00 to disable). The lines in SensorTag.py which do this are:

```python
    def enable(self):
        self.periph.writeCharacteristic(0x60, struct.pack('<bb', 0x01, 0x00))

    def disable(self):
        self.periph.writeCharacteristic(0x60, struct.pack('<bb', 0x00, 0x00))
```

where 0x60 is the handle for the Keypress characteristic configuration.

> I'm still curious as to why this fixes the issue. I read through some of the ctypes documentation but it is over my head right now. If you can give me an ELI5 explanation I'd really appreciate it. 

`ctypes` is for Python code to communicate with environments that expect C, such was Windows' API win32. There are a lot of differences between C and Python, such as features present in one side but not in the other. This stdlib module offers functions like `byref(obj)` that behave like C references, and primitive types like `c_int`, to convert Python numbers to stuff that C expects.

Manually converting objects is boring, and any mistakes will likely lead to segfaults or worse memory corruption. So `ctypes` allows you to annotate the Python objects that represent C functions, with the expected arguments and return types ([example](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/blob/f1d10936c507f141f80e72333d2b0d4f22f48f73/keyboard/_winkeyboard.py#L84)). So you can declare that some existing C function takes an integer and a pointer to a function that takes a long and a pointer to a buffer... and so on.

In this issue, it seems that one of the arguments of `SetWindowsHookEx` was declared, in Python, to be another function, with certain arguments and return types (`LowLevelMouseProc`). But what was actually passed was a function with different arguments and return type.s It's hard to know more details because it just reports as `CFunctionType`. This is most likely an issue in the type-checking itself, and not the type-checked code.

Almost done implementing this, and ran into a couple of interesting points.  I expected, since the PCPs are equivalent, that they should get perfect scores on all metrics.  This turns out to not be correct:

- The `majmin(_inv)` metrics give 0 score here, even with extension reduction. It turns out that majmin gets this right, but in the wrong way.  Specifically, it checks for exact quality agreement in the first 8 bins against the `maj` or `min` templates.  Both chords fail on this, so it gets flagged as a "no-chord".

  You could make the case that this chord is neither major nor minor, but the fact that one is notated as a dom7 (major-like) and the other as min7 (minor) seems like some evidence that they should be interpreted differently.  However, that would require rewriting the `majmin` metrics to be more quality-aware, and I'm not exactly keen to do that.  I'm fine with leaving it as is.

- The `sevenths(_inv)` metrics also give 0 score.  I was looking into the code, and this part (7th qualities)[https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/chord.py#L1275] seems weird. It's missing `dim7` and `hdim7` qualities... i wonder if this is a bug?  (Note, that's independent of the current issue)

  Anyway, the problem here is that it's looking for exact semitone agreement with one of the known qualities, which of course we don't have in either of these chords, but the fact that they're weird in the same way suggests that this behavior is wrong.  (Maybe @ejhumphrey or @mattmcvicar will fight me on this? :grin:)

I think the above can be resolved generally by relaxing the bin comparison strategy.  Rather than seeing if the chord(s) in question match any of the known templates, we can instead specify a mask vector `m` that indicates which semitones matter for the evaluation in question (in this case, root + m3, M3, TT, P5, m7, M7), and then check if `ref_semitones * m == est_semitones * m`.

I've implemented that kind of logic before in `pumpp`'s chord vocabulary reduction algorithm, and afaik it's correct.  It would probably change the behavior of the metrics, even when extension reduction is disabled, so it's worth having a conversation about.

IMO, the current behavior is incorrect/not expected/probably a bug, but I'm open to other interpretations.

> there is a vague idea that every evaluator function in each submodule should take reference and estimated annotations in the same format, and this breaks that. But, I could be convinced otherwise.

`segment` already breaks that since we re-merged the boundary detection and structure annotation metrics.  (The latter require labels, the former do not.)

> Wait, which one is the reference and which is the estimate?

I considered the two examples I gave to be references for two different songs: One that starts with 1.2 seconds of silence, and one that starts with music right away.

> And would you still think 1.2 is trivial if the label was `crowd noise` instead of `silence`?

I don't necessarily think 1.2 is trivial. I thought the idea of `trim=True` was to treat silence->nonsilence and nonsilence->silence as not meaningful, because they're easy to detect and have a significant influence on results.

The problem I see comes from confounding two things: The beginning/end of a file, and the beginning/end of the music. In some cases, they coincide, and in some cases they don't. This is why I think these cases need to be distinguished in the evaluation. It's a separate question of whether detecting the beginning/end of the music is meaningful or not (I'd say it is, but it can be instructive to compare algorithms on their performance of detecting boundaries within the music). "Detecting" the beginning/end of a file should not be relevant for anything.

def disk_io_counters():
    """Return disk I/O statistics for every disk installed on the
    system as a dict of raw tuples.
    """
    # determine partitions we want to look for
    def get_partitions():
        partitions = []
        with open_text("%s/partitions" % get_procfs_path()) as f:
            lines = f.readlines()[2:]
        for line in reversed(lines):
            _, _, _, name = line.split()
            if name[-1].isdigit():
                # we're dealing with a partition (e.g. 'sda1'); 'sda' will
                # also be around but we want to omit it
                partitions.append(name)
            else:
                if not partitions or not partitions[-1].startswith(name):
                    # we're dealing with a disk entity for which no
                    # partitions have been defined (e.g. 'sda' but
                    # 'sda1' was not around), see:
                    # https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/issues/338
                    partitions.append(name)
        return partitions

    retdict = {}
    partitions = get_partitions()
    with open_text("%s/diskstats" % get_procfs_path()) as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
    for line in lines:
        # OK, this is a bit confusing. The format of /proc/diskstats can
        # have 3 variations.
        # On Linux 2.4 each line has always 15 fields, e.g.:
        # "3     0   8 hda 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8"
        # On Linux 2.6+ each line *usually* has 14 fields, and the disk
        # name is in another position, like this:
        # "3    0   hda 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8"
        # ...unless (Linux 2.6) the line refers to a partition instead
        # of a disk, in which case the line has less fields (7):
        # "3    1   hda1 8 8 8 8"
        # See:
        # https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/iostats.txt
        # https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/ABI/testing/procfs-diskstats
        fields = line.split()
        fields_len = len(fields)
        if fields_len == 15:
            # Linux 2.4
            name = fields[3]
            reads = int(fields[2])
            (reads_merged, rbytes, rtime, writes, writes_merged,
                wbytes, wtime, _, busy_time, _) = map(int, fields[4:14])
        elif fields_len == 14:
            # Linux 2.6+, line referring to a disk
            name = fields[2]
            (reads, reads_merged, rbytes, rtime, writes, writes_merged,
                wbytes, wtime, _, busy_time, _) = map(int, fields[3:14])
        elif fields_len == 7:
            # Linux 2.6+, line referring to a partition
            name = fields[2]
            reads, rbytes, writes, wbytes = map(int, fields[3:])
            rtime = wtime = reads_merged = writes_merged = busy_time = 0
        else:
            raise ValueError("not sure how to interpret line %r" % line)

        if name in partitions:
            ssize = get_sector_size(name)
            rbytes *= ssize
            wbytes *= ssize
            retdict[name] = (reads, writes, rbytes, wbytes, rtime, wtime,
                             reads_merged, writes_merged, busy_time)
    return retdict
```
3.x:

```python
def disk_io_counters():
    """Return disk I/O statistics for every disk installed on the
    system as a dict of raw tuples.
    """
    # man iostat states that sectors are equivalent with blocks and
    # have a size of 512 bytes since 2.4 kernels. This value is
    # needed to calculate the amount of disk I/O in bytes.
    SECTOR_SIZE = 512

    # determine partitions we want to look for
    partitions = []
    with open_text("%s/partitions" % get_procfs_path()) as f:
        lines = f.readlines()[2:]
    for line in reversed(lines):
        _, _, _, name = line.split()
        if name[-1].isdigit():
            # we're dealing with a partition (e.g. 'sda1'); 'sda' will
            # also be around but we want to omit it
            partitions.append(name)
        else:
            if not partitions or not partitions[-1].startswith(name):
                # we're dealing with a disk entity for which no
                # partitions have been defined (e.g. 'sda' but
                # 'sda1' was not around), see:
                # https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/issues/338
                partitions.append(name)
    #
    retdict = {}
    with open_text("%s/diskstats" % get_procfs_path()) as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
    for line in lines:
        # http://www.mjmwired.net/kernel/Documentation/iostats.txt
        fields = line.split()
        if len(fields) > 7:
            _, _, name, reads, _, rbytes, rtime, writes, _, wbytes, wtime = \
                fields[:11]
        else:
            # from kernel 2.6.0 to 2.6.25
            _, _, name, reads, rbytes, writes, wbytes = fields
            rtime, wtime = 0, 0
        if name in partitions:
            rbytes = int(rbytes) * SECTOR_SIZE
            wbytes = int(wbytes) * SECTOR_SIZE
            reads = int(reads)
            writes = int(writes)
            rtime = int(rtime)
            wtime = int(wtime)
            retdict[name] = (reads, writes, rbytes, wbytes, rtime, wtime)
    return retdict
```

I run the webhook bot locally, but you can't use `localhost` for that. to do that you need to first obtain your public ip address, than make sure you are not behind a NAT (and if you are configure port forwarding as needed). if you are not familiar with networking concepts needed or don't have access to do these i suggest using a server[1](#myfootnote1) or if you don't want to expend money you can use [c9.io](http://c9.io) , which is a free service that allows you to run your code online for testing purposes without the hassle of configuring a local environment. otherwise here are the steps to run a webhook bot locally: 1. get your public ip address * hint: it does not start with 192.x.x.x or 127.x.x.x, and its not localhost * you can search google for "what is my ip" (note: you need to disable proxies before doing that) * note that depending on your isp it may change each time you restart your modem, so watch out for that and update accordingly the new ip on your bot * **\*optional*** if your ip changes you can use a dynamic dns service such as [noip](http://www.noip.com/) to prevent the need for modifying it each time 2. run a webserver at desired port to test if your computer is accessible. - you can run the flask example without the line that sets the webhook - you can ask a friend to open https://YOUR_PUBLIC_IP or simply use [this site](http://www.yougetsignal.com/tools/open-ports/) to check, put 8443 or 443 based on the port you chose while testing - if your port was open than that's all, you can uncomment the webhook line and it works 3. if your port was closed: forward ports from WAN to your LAN ip - first check for firewalls on your pc, maybe you don't need to forward your port and flask is blocked by an antivirus or firewall - if that was not the case follow these steps: 1. get your private ip address - you can use `ifconfig` in terminal on linux or `ipconfig` in cmd on windows to find it , it most likely starts with 192.168.x.x and is in front of `IPv4 Address` 2. go to your modem's configuration and Forward desired port - you have to forward the port (e.g. 8443) to your local ip, since each modem has a different configuration i can't describe this step but there is an excelent website that covers most modem configurations : [portforward.com](http://portforward.com/english/applications/port_forwarding/Apache/default.htm). just select your modem model and you get a picture guide for port forwarding. - depending on your network configuration you may get a new local ip for your pc on each restart, so if it stopped working try checking to see if your local ip has changed and edit your port forward accordingly 4. check to see your port is [open now](http://www.yougetsignal.com/tools/open-ports/). - if it still was not open you may need to restart your modem for configuration to take effect. - try disabling your firewall temporarily to test if that's causing the issue - if you are using a vpn/proxy you need to disconnect it for this to work you can read more about this here: - http://superuser.com/a/961846/248136 - http://lifehacker.com/127276/geek-to-live--how-to-access-a-home-server-behind-a-routerfirewall - http://www.thetechmentor.com/posts/how-to-host-a-web-site-from-your-computer/ _some of these link also include downloading/setting up a webserver , you can skip that since you already have flask just look at the parts about firewall/router_ [1]: there are very cheap options available for a server, Digital ocean for example takes only 5$ per month

Look, once you set a netns for a process, you **can not** leave it. So there is only one way: fork a child and run setns in the child. No other solution. It's not a library feature, but the OS design.

Pls notice, that if you instantiate `IPDB` or `IPRoute` within a process running in a netns, you do not need to use `NetNS(…)` or `IPDB(nl=NetNS(…))` — any instantiated `IPDB` or `IPRoute` will operate in the netns of the process.

So this pull enables functionality as seen in the (new) readme:

```
with colorama(Fore.RED):
    print('some red text')
    with colorama(Back.GREEN):
        print('and with a green background')
        with colorama(Style.DIM):
            print('and in dim text'):
print('back to normal now')
```

This is probably because the users of `PyFunceble` run it in a subprocess, where "stdout" is not a tty, so colorama does not recognize stdout as a wrap-able stream and therefore strips the ANSI color codes.

Consider this simple script (let's put it in a file named "a.py"):
```python
import colorama
colorama.init()
print(colorama.Fore.RED + 'hello' + colorama.Fore.RESET)
```
And this script that executes "a.py" in a subprocess (let's call it "b.py")
```python
import subprocess
proc = subprocess.Popen("python a.py", stdout=subprocess.PIPE, shell=True)
output, error = proc.communicate()
print(output)
```
If you run the first script directly (`python a.py`) you will get a red "hello". If you run b.py, which runs a.py, you will get a "regular" non-red "hello". The stdout of the subprocess is a pipe, i.e. not a "terminal stream" (tty), so the colors will be stripped.
I can't think of a good work around for you. Technically this is the intended behavior.

The problem here IMO is that Maya, everytime it makes a conversion of a datetime to a timezone, takes the name in the tzinfo object and passes it to pytz to look up a new tzinfo object. That's unecessary, you'll just get the same object back, so it's a roundtrip with little point, and it also means it will only work with timezones in the Olsen database included in the pytz version you are running.

I don't know if there is a reason for that design, but that's what causing this problem.

class Color:

Usage:

I think the setable property is a no go if you ever want to support any form of data compression. In order to map from `pixel_array ` to `PixelData`, you will need to know the transfer syntax (ie the compression scheme), cols, rows, # bit used per pixel, etc. 

Assuming you don't want to require the user to have set those on the data set in advance, something like this makes sense:
`dataset.set_image(pixel_array, rows, cols, transfer_syntax,...)` where those values are then all set on the underlying dataset.

Well, I *do* think it's an issue, but alas one I don't have a proper solution for ;-)
First of all, there's no such thing as a "Mopidy timeout" - IIRC Mopidy (the server) will wait indefinitely on every backend to fulfill a request - any timeouts you may see are generated by the client (and may be configurable there).
IMHO, the proper "solution" would be for the Mopidy API to provide *pagination*, i.e. allow clients to pass `limit` and `offset` parameters with search and other commands. That way, the client would control how many objects are retrieved, and could "split up" large queries into smaller parts and remain responsive.
This has been on the agenda for quite a while (just search Mopidy's open issues), but there hasn't been any progress on it AFAIK.
So, yes, I think it *is* an issue, but none that mopidy-dleyna can solve properly, I think.
Therefore I'm closing this, as you suggested ;-)

This may not solve the problem, but I thought it worth mentioning.

In the past, when I've had issues with PIL/Pillow, it was because the host OS's copy of this library was interfering with the one in my virtualenv.  The solution for this is to:

* Create a virtualenv if you haven't already
* Create an empty file called `sitecustomize.py` in `<virtualenv>/lib/python2.7/` (replace python2.7 with whatever python version you're using)
* Use `pip` to install whatever packages you need, including `Pillow`.

You pay by the request, not by the tweet. So, I don't understand the problem with throwing away tweets. 

A worst case scenario would be if you wanted 501 tweets. In this case, you would have to pay for two requests, assuming you are downloading the maximum per request (500). 

Or, let's say you wanted 400 tweets. You could can set `maxResults` to 400 and not throw away any tweets, or you can set it to 500 and throw away 100 tweets. In either case, you pay for one request.

The write_long function takes an 32-bit integer value, splits it into to 16-bit values and writes them to two registers. In your case it'll try to write to register 2008 and 2009.

But according to

> action = write, size = 1, type = Uint16, possible values = 1-4

it looks to me that you should only write to a single register. My guess is that the error might come from trying to write to register 2009.

Try using write_register instead of write_long to only write one 16-bit integer to register 2008 and not touch register 2009:

```
instrument.write_register(2008, 1)
```

I just found https://github.com/Microsoft/vcpkg/tree/master/ports/freetype (see my freetype-devel post). I imported the 0001 patch.

The build worked without MingW, but thanks for the info Hin-Tak, you never know when you're going to need it again :)

So, the sequence of commands for using VS and CMake on Windows is:

1. Download and unpack FreeType and Harfbuzz tarballs
2. cd FreeType-dir
    1. `mkdir build; cd build`
    2. Import 0001 patch
    3. `cmake -DWITH_HarfBuzz=OFF -DWITH_PNG=OFF -DWITH_BZip2=OFF -DWITH_ZLIB=OFF -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX="C:\FullPath\tmp\prefix\" ..`
    4. `cmake --build . --config Release --target install`
3. cd Harfbuzz-dir
    1. `mkdir build; cd build`
    2. `cmake -DHB_HAVE_FREETYPE=ON -DHB_HAVE_GLIB=OFF -DCMAKE_PREFIX_PATH="C:\FullPath\tmp\prefix\" -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX="C:\FullPath\tmp\prefix\" ..`
    3. `cmake --build . --config Release --target install`
4. cd FreeType-dir/build
    1. `cmake -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS:BOOL=true -DWITH_HarfBuzz=ON -DWITH_PNG=OFF -DWITH_BZip2=OFF -DWITH_ZLIB=OFF -DHARFBUZZ_INCLUDE_DIRS="C:\FullPath\tmp\prefix\include\harfbuzz" -DCMAKE_PREFIX_PATH="C:\FullPath\tmp\prefix\" -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX="C:\FullPath\tmp\prefix\" ..`
    2. `cmake --build . --config Release --target install`

This will create a `freetype.dll` in `C:\FullPath\tmp\prefix\bin`. :)

The problem is rather simple to detect. Comparing more than 2000 responses with lexical comparison makes it super slow. Nothing to do with MongoDB. 

A solution can be:
compute the word vectors for each statement to determine the vector for the whole phrase.
Rinse and repeat for the user input. The only thing we then having to do is query the vectors in MongoDB on closest similarity (Cosine Similarity). This will also be more accurate than lexical comparison.

Drawback:
Loading in a good and accurate word embeddings model into memory will take time.

Some tips on where to start Google for word2vec, doc2vec, glove, fast text.

Yes, this library is needed. **DO NOT** pass user input as a `kwarg` or using `data` param to a form because it is not validated!

`data` is meant to be used as a backup/default value for a field and is not run against your validators. Also, the value from `obj` takes precedence over `data`, so your api resources won't update your fields with the new value passed in a PUT request if you also pass an existing `obj` to the form.

Given repos A, B and C:
* `A` has `B` as submodule in `externals/B`.
* `B` has `C` as submodule in `externals/C`.

This should give the following tree:
``` 
A
└── A.txt
└── externals
    └── B
        └── externals
            └──  C
                └──  C.txt
        └── .gitattributes
        └── .gitmodules
        └── B.txt
└── .gitmodules
```

If `B` sets `externals/C` as `export-ignore` it won't be in the tarball, if `B` is exported. However, if `A` is exported, `C` is in the tarball of `A`, as my patch from #40 did use the full path of the submodule from the root (i.e. `externals/B/externals/C`) instead of the one relative to the submodule defining the ignore (`B`). Same goes for the file ignore patch in #18: the full repo path is checked instead of the relative one. Please wait a sec to let me fix this.

Re `extra_args`

I’m using attrs syntax for simplicity here now, but you could do this:

```python
@attr.s
class C:
    x  = attr.ib()
    parent = attr.ib()
    tmp = attr.ib(init=False)

    def __attrs_post_init__(self):
        self.tmp = TempStorage()
        self.tmp.link(self.parent)
```

The advantage is that it doesn’t introduce any new concepts.  If you don’t want that field to be there, make it private using an underscore.  OTOH I learned the hard way that trying to pander to every single use case under the sun leads to regret and tears. :)  You can always write your own `__init__`.

Also this seems like a feature that can be added later if really necessary.

I just discussed this with @gvanrossum. We're going to go with option 1, as advocated by @ilevkivskyi: `field()` will have 2 parameters `default` and `default_factory` (in addition to the existing parameters that `field()` provides). The stub for `field()` will have 3 overloads:

- The type of `field(default=value, ...)` will be the type of `value`.
- The type of `field(default_factory=callable, ...)` will be the type of `callable()`.
- The type of `field(...)` (with neither `default` nor `default_factory` specified) will be `Any`.

(Here, `...` signifies other parameters to `field()`, such as `init`, `cmp`, `hash`, etc.)

Providing both `default` and `default_factory` will be an error that `field()` will catch and raise a `ValueError`.

For me, the two big features of `as_dict()` are:

1. Recursion: a dataclass containing a list of other dataclasses, for example, and being able to create nested dicts from that.

1. The ability to specify a different dict factory, specifically `collections.OrderedDict`. It would be a shame if there were no way to create an `OrderedDict` from an ordered collection of fields (which is what a dataclass is).

I think there are a few related issues here:

1. When a TCP connection unexpectedly drops, there's no exception to notify application code of the error.
2. When the TCP connection has dropped, you can't manually `close` a connection.
3. When the TCP connection has dropped, you can't manually `close` a channel.
4. When the TCP connection has dropped, you can't unsubscribe existing consumers.

Regarding point 1. Error handling in async code is generally quite complicated (where should the exception go if all your application code is currently idle?). `asyncio` approaches the issue by allowing you to register an [exception handler](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-eventloop.html#asyncio.BaseEventLoop.set_exception_handler) with the event loop - so in theory all `asynqp` needs to do is raise an appropriate exception when the connection is lost, and then you can use the loop's exception handler to build any application-specific retry logic.

I happen to know that the transport calls [`Protocol.connection_lost`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-protocol.html#asyncio.BaseProtocol.connection_lost) when the socket dies. It looks like [our `Protocol`](https://github.com/benjamin-hodgson/asynqp/blob/master/src/asynqp/protocol.py) doesn't override that method. I think all it needs to do is `raise ConnectionLost from exc` - I'll accept a pull request ([with tests](https://github.com/benjamin-hodgson/asynqp/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md)) which addresses that (I may get some time to work on it tomorrow but I can't make any promises).

Points 2-4 seem related to me (though I split them up because they may require separate fixes). Thinking out loud - I guess that those three methods try to send a frame over the wire to the server, which blocks forever because the socket is dead. I suppose one option would be to throw an exception when you try to send data through the protocol if the connection has been lost, and do some sort of "emergency shut-down" of any live objects or tasks which assume the connection is still open.

In general, `asynqp`'s error handling capabilities have room for improvement. Thanks very much for raising the issue :smiley:

Here's a semi-hacked-together example using gevent and the `-progress` param @Depau mentioned:
```python
import gevent.monkey; gevent.monkey.patch_all()

import contextlib
import ffmpeg
import gevent
import os
import shutil
import socket
import subprocess
import sys
import tempfile


@contextlib.contextmanager
def _tmpdir_scope():
    tmpdir = tempfile.mkdtemp()
    try:
        yield tmpdir
    finally:
        shutil.rmtree(tmpdir)


def _watch_progress(filename, sock, handler):
    connection, client_address = sock.accept()
    data = ''
    try:
        while True:
            more_data = connection.recv(16)
            if not more_data:
                break
            data += more_data
            lines = data.split('\n')
            for line in lines[:-1]:
                parts = line.split('=')
                key = parts[0] if len(parts) > 0 else None
                value = parts[1] if len(parts) > 1 else None
                handler(key, value)
            data = lines[-1]
    finally:
        connection.close()


@contextlib.contextmanager
def watch_progress(handler):
    with _tmpdir_scope() as tmpdir:
        filename = os.path.join(tmpdir, 'sock')
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_UNIX, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        with contextlib.closing(sock):
            sock.bind(filename)
            sock.listen(1)
            child = gevent.spawn(_watch_progress, filename, sock, handler)
            try:
                yield filename
            except:
                gevent.kill(child)
                raise


duration = float(ffmpeg.probe('in.mp4')['format']['duration'])

prev_text = None

def handler(key, value):
    global prev_text
    if key == 'out_time_ms':
        text = '{:.02f}%'.format(float(value) / 10000. / duration)
        if text != prev_text:
            print(text)
            prev_text = text


with watch_progress(handler) as filename:
    p = subprocess.Popen(
        (ffmpeg
            .input('in.mp4')
            .output('out.mp4')
            .global_args('-progress', 'unix://{}'.format(filename))
            .overwrite_output()
            .compile()
        ),
        stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
    )
    out = p.communicate()

if p.returncode != 0:
    sys.stderr.write(out[1])
    sys.exit(1)
```

- It creates and listens on a unix-domain socket and passes that to ffmpeg.
- The watcher runs in its own gevent greenlet, but it could be changed to use actual threads instead if desired.
- In order to know the progress percentage, it uses the duration reported by ffprobe, since the normal progress info doesn't seem to contain such info.

Excellent bug report. Unfortunately, a satisfactory solution is going to be
difficult.

My understanding is that exists() should also handle the case of a
directory. Unfortunately, on S3, directories don't ever exist! This means
that we can only tell if a directory exists if at least one key starts with
the directory prefix.

However, the more I think about it, the more the current solution is
broken. After all, a key of foo/bar will currently return exists() = true
for "f", "fo", "foo", "foo/" etc, which is clearly wrong.

How about this: For a key of "foo", it will try to head_object for "foo"
and "foo/". If either return true, then the file or directory exists.

Unfortunately, that means two head_requests to ensure a missing file is
actually missing. The best algorithm I can come up with is:

   1. If the key ends with "/", return whether if it exists.
   2. Otherwise, check if the key exists. If it does, return true,
   otherwise, goto 1.

This means that things that are obviously directories only need 1 head
request. Things that might be files need 1 head request if it exists, and
two if it does not.

On 20 June 2017 at 16:14, Ken Cochrane <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> Depending on what you have for file names, some of the files are not
> getting copied over correctly.
>
> If you have the following two files:
>
>    - assets/bootstrap/css/bootstrap.min.css.map
>    - assets/bootstrap/css/bootstrap.min.css
>
> It will copy over the first one bootstrap.min.css.map but it will not
> copy over the second one bootstrap.min.css. This is because of the way we
> determine if a file exists or not.
>
> Look at the exists method found here: https://github.com/etianen/
> django-s3-storage/blob/master/django_s3_storage/storage.py#L269
>
>     def exists(self, name):
>         # We also need to check for directory existence, so we'll list matching
>         # keys and return success if any match.
>         results = self.s3_connection.list_objects_v2(
>             Bucket=self.settings.AWS_S3_BUCKET_NAME,
>             MaxKeys=1,
>             Prefix=self._get_key_name(name),
>         )
>         return bool(results["KeyCount"])
>
> What this method does is it will do a listing of the bucket using the
> prefix of the given name. When it wants to see if assets/bootstrap/css/
> bootstrap.min.css exists it will list the contents of the bucket, using
> the prefix of assets/bootstrap/css/bootstrap.min.css and return at most 1
> key.
>
> This method will return True even if assets/bootstrap/css/
> bootstrap.min.css isn't there if the assets/bootstrap/css/
> bootstrap.min.css.map file is there because the prefix of
> assets/bootstrap/css/bootstrap.min.css matches the file
> assets/bootstrap/css/bootstrap.min.css.map. So you end up with a false
> positive. If the files doesn't exists, it will use the
> get_available_name() method from here: https://github.com/django/
> django/blob/master/django/core/files/storage.py#L60 to generate a random
> file name and store that.
>
> What happens is that the file is copied over, but the name was changed,
> and thus is unavailable when you try and fetch from s3 directly.
>
> The fix is to do something like this.
>
>     def exists(self, name):
>         try:
>             self.s3_connection.head_object(
>                 Bucket=self.settings.AWS_S3_BUCKET_NAME,
>                 Key=self._get_key_name(name))
>             return True
>         except ClientError as e:
>             if e.response['Error']['Code'] == "404":
>                 return False
>             else:
>                 raise e
>
> This code makes a head call for the object, and if it returns with no
> error, you know it exists, if it returns an error, check to make sure it is
> a 404, and not another error. If a 404, then we know the file doesn't
> exists.
>
> Similar code can be found here: https://github.com/jschneier/
> django-storages/blob/master/storages/backends/s3boto3.py#L452-L467 and
> https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33842944/check-if-a-
> key-exists-in-a-bucket-in-s3-using-boto3
>
> I can submit a PR for the fix, but I had one question around the comment
> in the exists method.
>
>   # We also need to check for directory existence, so we'll list matching
>   # keys and return success if any match.
>
> It says that we need to also check for directory existence. My code from
> above doesn't work for directories, so we will need a solution for that use
> case. Do you know if that comment is still relevant?

> I wonder if we should introduce an option to disable fsync calls for situations where performance is more important and just atomicity (not much more) is required.

You will loose atomicity if you do that. It may be possible that the new file name gets written to disk before the data is fully written. This only provides protection against errors where writing to the temporary file fails (e.g. out of disk space).

If you want to include fsync'ing of the directory, you could make it optional. Maybe durability is out of scope.

```python
def redirect_preserve_filters(self, request, obj, change_list=False):
        opts = self.model._meta
        preserved_filters = self.get_preserved_filters(request)
        if change_list:
            redir_url = reverse('admin:%s_%s_changelist' %
                                (opts.app_label, opts.model_name),
                                current_app=self.admin_site.name)
        else:
            pk_value = obj._get_pk_val()
            redir_url = reverse('admin:%s_%s_change' %
                                (opts.app_label, opts.model_name),
                                args=(pk_value,),
                                current_app=self.admin_site.name)
        preserved_filters = self.get_preserved_filters(request)
        redir_url = add_preserved_filters({'preserved_filters': preserved_filters, 'opts': opts}, redir_url)
        return HttpResponseRedirect(redir_url)

I don't recall seeing the JavaScript client sending a CLOSE packet, that is why I did not add it here. Its this in response to a disconnect call on the client?

> And then again it only happens when I have multiple threads running that try to accept the client messages.

Can you explain this statement in detail? Are you talking about server-side threads or client-side?

I am investigating an issue that is possibly related: https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-engineio/pull/51

The test that you are running could be exposing an issue that is also reported in link above, but note that what you are doing is not representative of the normal usage for Socket.IO. There is really no point to constantly connect and disconnect from the server, the idea is that clients establish long running connections to the server. The constant connections and disconnections are expensive, this is a protocol that is optimized for connection that last a long time.

Giving an absolute "mps" measure is really impossible, as there are a lot of factors that affect the performance of the server. There is a "latency.py" example in this repository that you can use to test the latency for a round-trip message from client to server and back. The idea is that you deploy this application to your production server and that way you can measure actual latency in your production configuration.

While digging into the code I could figure how to pass to `formset_factory()` (and other variations like inlineformset_factory(), ...) function the `formset` parameter so that you can implements a custom validation as in https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/forms/formsets/#custom-formset-validation.

So you need to implement your custom validation in a base formset class which would looks as follow for `InlineFormset`:
```
MyCustomBaseInlineFormset(forms.models.BaseInlineFormSet):
    def clean(self):
        # code here...
        pass
```
Then in the `InlineFormSet` you assign the above class to the `formset_class` attribute:
```
class MyInlineFormset(InlineFormSet):
    model = my_model
    form_class = MyForm
    formset_class = MyCustomBaseInlineFormset   # which will be passed into the formset argument of inlineformset_factory().
```

I went into the websocket_server.py file and modified
```
def run_forever(self):
        try:
            logger.info("Listening on port %d for clients.." % self.port)

```

to this
```
def run_forever(self):
        try:
            logger.info("Listening on port %d for clients.." % self.port)
            # self.serve_forever()
            _serve = self.serve_forever
            # Start a thread with the server -- that thread will then start one
            # more thread for each request
            server_thread = threading.Thread(target=_serve)
            # Exit the server thread when the main thread terminates
            server_thread.daemon = True
            server_thread.start()
```

Benchmark script:
```Python
import mxnet as mx
import sys
import os
import scipy
import numpy as np
from mxnet.test_utils import assert_almost_equal
import time

def measure_cost(repeat, f, *args, **kwargs):
    # start bench
    start = time.time()
    results = []
    for i in range(repeat):
        results.append(f(*args, **kwargs))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def measure_fallback(repeat, a):
    # start bench
    start = time.time()
    results = []
    for i in range(repeat):
        results.append(a.tostype('default'))
    for result in results:
        result.wait_to_read()
    end = time.time()
    diff = end - start
    return diff / repeat

def main():
    shape_lhs = (35000, 256)
    shape_rhs = (35000, 35000)
    dns = np.random.uniform(size=shape_lhs)
    mx_dns = mx.nd.array(dns, ctx=mx.gpu())
    mx_dns_cpy = mx_dns.copy()
    for density in [0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001]:
        csr = scipy.sparse.random(shape_lhs[0], shape_lhs[1], density=density, format = 'csr', dtype=np.float32)
        mx_csr = mx.nd.sparse.csr_matrix((csr.data, csr.indices, csr.indptr), shape=shape_rhs, ctx=mx.gpu())
        mx_csr_dns = mx_csr.tostype('default')
        sparse_cost = 0.0
        dns_cost = 0.0
        mx.nd.waitall()
        #warmup
        check = mx.nd.dot(mx_csr, mx_dns, transpose_a=True, forward_stype='default')
        check_np = np.dot(mx_csr_dns.asnumpy().T, dns)
        assert_almost_equal(check.asnumpy(), check_np, atol=1e-5, rtol=1e-4)
        print(check.shape)
        mx.nd.waitall()
        for i in range(50):
            sparse_cost += measure_cost(1, mx.nd.dot, mx_csr, mx_dns, transpose_a=True, forward_stype='default')
            dns_cost += measure_cost(1, mx.nd.dot, mx_csr_dns, mx_dns, transpose_a=True)
        print("%.2f %%" % (density*100), dns_cost / sparse_cost)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```
Benchmark results on single K80 GPU (all speedup values are based on comparison to the same dense dot computation):
(density of CSR matrix - speedup before the change / speedup after the change)
1.00% - 10.641220445769855 / 77.02531563623299

```python
import mxnet as mx

class Test(mx.gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, input_size, output_size, **kwargs):
        super(Test, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        self.input_size = input_size
        self.output_size = output_size
        self.hidden_unit_size = output_size*input_size

        self.num_cells = 2
        with self.name_scope():
            self.cell_a = mx.gluon.rnn.GRUCell(self.hidden_unit_size, input_size=input_size)
            self.cell_b = mx.gluon.rnn.GRUCell(self.hidden_unit_size, input_size=input_size)

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, inputs, states):
        prev_h = states[0]
        if F is mx.symbol:
            prev_h = F.split(prev_h, axis=0, num_outputs=self.num_cells, squeeze_axis=1)

        cell_a_next_h, _ = self.cell_a(inputs, [prev_h[0]])

        cell_b_next_h, _ = self.cell_b(prev_h[1], [prev_h[1]])

        b_output = cell_b_next_h.reshape(shape=(0, self.input_size, self.output_size))

        return cell_a_next_h, b_output, []

    def state_info(self, batch_size=0):
        return [{'shape': (self.num_cells, batch_size, self.hidden_unit_size), '__layout__': 'LNC'}]

    def begin_state(self, batch_size=0, func=mx.ndarray.zeros, **kwargs):
        states = []
        for i, info in enumerate(self.state_info(batch_size)):
            if info is not None:
                info.update(kwargs)
            else:
                info = kwargs
            states.append(func(name='%sh0_%d'%(self.prefix, i), **info))
        return states

args_nof_examples = 1
args_nof_batches = 1
args_batch_size = 1

args_input_size = 1
args_output_size = 1

data = mx.ndarray.zeros(shape=(args_nof_examples, args_input_size))
labels = mx.ndarray.ones((args_nof_examples, args_input_size))
gen = mx.io.NDArrayIter(data, labels, args_batch_size, last_batch_handle='discard')

with mx.cpu(0) as context:
    model = Test(args_input_size, args_output_size)
    model.initialize(mx.init.Xavier(), ctx = context)
    model.hybridize()

    loss = mx.gluon.loss.SoftmaxCrossEntropyLoss()

    states = model.begin_state(args_batch_size)
    for batch in gen:
        with mx.autograd.record():
            a, b, _ = model(batch.data[0], states)
            L = loss(b, batch.label[0])
        L.backward()
```

Are we sure that your first test is actually testing the right thing? Your comment says it won't clear because the other TI is running, but I think that TI has already ended and it's not clearing because of the `mark_success` flag. Maybe just a clarification.

If I understood your point about try number correctly (i.e. you don't like the idea of adding it to the task_instance table), then I really, really feel try number is critical to Airflow regardless (we were talking about it with Alex/Max internally + having a separate table that stores all task instance tries append-only).

Here is the expert from my mailing list email in case you missed it.
```

The concept of "attempt" is needed regardless of logging, the way retries are stored/handled right now is not very sane.
- Old TI state is permanently deleted
- In the task logs you get "Try 1/6"... 2/6... 3/6... 1/6... 2/6 in logs which doesn't make sense (if it's the Nth time the task is running it should be logged as the Nth time). I recall other strange behaviors in these log lines too (maybe something like Try 4/3).
- The "primary key" for a task instance run is not complete (which is what Allison's logging change needs), you could say that TaskInstance should only keep track of the latest TaskInstance run, but we still want to store all tries for a task instance somewhere in the database, and we still need to key this off of "attempt".
```
I don't like start_dates, there aren't really guarantees around them being completely unique (which try_number gives us for free), they are a poor approximation for a unique key. Feel free to reply to the open source thread and we can discuss along with Max/Alex/other committers though.

I hope everyone is on Python 2.7, but if it's just a few lines of code that need fixing to keep 2.6 support, then that is a good thing to do.

Realistically, core Plone should still be fine for use with 2.6, but lots of add-ons can inadvertently have added 2.7-only code, so using 2.6 *is* getting more dangerous and unsupported  every day.

It had recently crossed my mind to see if I could support `asyncio` but I hadn't put any real thought into it.  I'm by no means an expert in asynchronous python.  It is great that you have put some real thought into how it might work.

I'm inclined to explore if we could roll async support into the core library (option 1. above) but I don't yet have a clear sense of what that entails. I'm quite fond of the current API and I wouldn't want to do it if it ended up polluting it with clunky options or an undue expansion of its surface.

A few questions off the top of my head:

1. Could the API potentially be exactly the same and the implementation switching based only on whether the the target and handlers functions are coroutines?  Or would we need something more explicit?
2. Do you know of any other libraries that support something similar? i.e. is there a best practice out there already for this sort of thing?
3. If the decorated target is a coroutine, does that imply that the handlers have to be too? Can those be mix and matched?
4. Some keywords args can also be callable so their values can be evaluated at runtime. Would we have to or want to support async for those as well?

Hi @erikyao,

When you use `session.run`, two messages `run` and `pull-all` will be sent to the server. The moment the server receives these two messages, the server will automatically creates a transaction and run the cypher statement inside this transaction. 

It is true that session.run will not ensure that your transaction is finished when run returns, while if you consume all your session run result or close your session, then it will be guaranteed to have your transaction finished. And it is highly recommended to always consume results and close sessions. You could also find more docs [here](https://neo4j.com/docs/developer-manual/current/drivers/sessions-transactions/)

Cheers,
Zhen

    def _build_pig_cmd(self):
        opts = self.pig_options()

        if self.pig_parameters():
            with open('pig_param_file', 'w') as param_file:
                param_file.writelines(["%s=%s%s" % (k, v, os.linesep) for (k, v) in six.iteritems(self.pig_parameters())])
            opts.append('-param_file')
            opts.append('pig_param_file')

        if self.pig_properties():
            with open('pig_property_file', 'w') as prop_file:
                prop_file.writelines(["%s=%s%s" % (k, v, os.linesep) for (k, v) in six.iteritems(self.pig_properties())])
            opts.append('-propertyFile')
            opts.append('pig_property_file')

        cmd = [self.pig_command_path()] + opts + ["-f", self.pig_script_path()]

        logger.info(subprocess.list2cmdline(cmd))
        return cmd

Apparently whatever stack `boto` is using for Python 3.3 doesn't support this feature? I'm kinda surprised because `pip` on Python 3 evidently supports it, since `pip -r requirements.txt` works just fine in `dateutil`'s test suite.

This may be a specific problem with `wheel` on Python 3.3, possibly this feature was added after `wheel` dropped support for Python 3.3?

One albeit annoying way to handle this is to go back to using a `sys.version` conditional, but conditional only on Python 3.3. That way people will only have a problem if they use Python 2.6 to install a wheel created using Python 3.3, which is fairly unlikely.

```python
requires = [
    'docutils>=0.10',
    'jmespath>=0.7.1,<1.0.0',
]

# Python 3.3 was failing to build the wheels with python_version specifiers
if sys.version_info[:2] == (3, 3):
    requires += ['python-dateutil>=2.1,<3.0.0']
else:
    requires += [
        # the latest version that supports python2.6 is 2.6.*
        'python-dateutil>=2.1,<2.7.0;python_version=="2.6"',
        'python-dateutil>=2.1,<3.0.0;python_version>="2.7"',
        # required for ordered dictionary support in python2.6
        'ordereddict==1.1;python_version=="2.6"',
        # requrired for a json version that allows specification of
        # ordereddict use instead of a normal dict for json objects
        # in python2.6
        'simplejson==3.3.0;python_version=="2.6"'
    ]
```

Hi @minibear0523 , the credit card is not charged in that case until you **capture** the payment.

The `approved` state of the `payment` resource means it does not require any further approval by the buyer.  If you look at the `related_resources` included in the `payment` resource, you should see an `authorization` resource.  This resource contains the pertinent information relating to the payment authorization (how long the authorization is valid, links to capture or void the payment, and a link back to the parent `payment` resource).  A credit card payment made with `intent` set to `authorization` will only charge the credit card once the payment is captured using the `authorization` ID.  

Here's an example (from [paypalrestsdk/payments.py](https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-Python-SDK/blob/master/paypalrestsdk/payments.py#L143)):

````python
>>> authorization = Authorization.find("<AUTHORIZATION_ID>")
>>> capture = authorization.capture({ "amount": { "currency": "USD", "total": "1.00" } })
>>> authorization.void() # return True or False
````

To see a flow of this process, refer to *[Capture payments later](https://developer.paypal.com/docs/integration/direct/capture-payment/)* on PayPal Developer.

This is possible (not with this syntax, of course, because TOML does not allow duplicate keys), but I don’t think it’s a good idea overall. It is better to wrap the package (IPython in this example) in a custom setup.py, and specify the conditional dependency there.

Something like:

```python
# ipython-select/setup.py
from setuptools import setup
setup(
    name='ipython-select',
    version='1.0',
    installs_requires=[
        'ipython>=6.3; python_version>="3"',
        'ipython<6; python_version<"3"',
    ],
)
```

```toml
# Pipfile

"pipenv with a conda backend" makes perfect sense in principle. Conda (unlike apt-get) provides all the basic concepts that pipenv is built on: the ability to create isolated environments, resolve package requirements into specific pinned versions and install them in those environments, etc. Of course every project has to make hard decisions about how to spend limited resources, and my guess is that this is outside the scope of what the pipenv devs want or are able to prioritize right now, but the idea is coherent :-).

In fact you can just use ctypes for this; see e.g. https://github.com/anntzer/mpl_cairo/blob/master/mpl_cairo/__init__.py (let me know if this is unclear).

The advantage of using ctypes is that you can write your C code as usual, without any dlopen() (which may be helpful when trying to keep things cross-platform); you just don't link the shared object when building a manylinux wheel and rely on ctypes to load the needed symbols globally before loading the extension module.

I can easily ignore things locally in git by adding them to `$HOME/.config/git/ignore` or `.git/info/exclude`, but it's hard to tell `pytest` to ignore things without changing something else that can't easily be ignored by `git` since `pytest` doesn't overlay configuration files. It only uses the first configuration file it finds.

The only workaround I found was to do run either:

 * `python setup.py test --addopt=--ignore=.c9`

Or

  * `PYTEST_ADDOPTS=--ignore=.c9 python setup.py test`

But those are cumbersome and hard to remember. And in the heat of running all tests it takes a good five minutes to understand why `pytest` is collecting more than it should. It happened me to both this project and to `pep8/pycodestyle`.

Did someone change / fix `zodb-cache-size-bytes` at some point?

The problem with `zodb-cache-size` is that it is very inaccurate: we have measured objects sizes from 0.6kb up to more than 70kb (catalog metadata). This makes it very inaccurate to calculate.

I think `zodb-cache-size-bytes` is ok when the expectations are correct: it actually just counts the estimated pickle size (I guess according to the data file position). And it is actually configured in kilobytes, not in bytes 😏.
As far as I observed it, the memory footprint is approx. 3x the `zodb-cache-size-bytes` (in kb); see fig. 4 in my comment above; that's probably the average difference between pickled size and size in memory.

Did you use the `zodb-cache-size-bytes` lately? How did it work out?

Regarding this change: it is not really relevant if the cache-size is configured in bytes or in amount of obbjects, it will work for both. It did only matter in finding the correct solution as I realized that our `zodb-cache-size` was not configured well for this hardware.

As I've been working on this some more, I think I prefer something like this:

```
class WordCount(Topology):
    word_spout = WordSpout(output_fields=['word'], parallelism_hint=2)
    word_count_bolt = WordCountBolt(inputs=[word_spout], group_on=['word'], parallelism_hint=8)

There are two main things about this approach:

1.  There's no intermediate `Spec` class you have to deal with, the `pystorm.Component` base class will accept these extra kwargs that are only used by the DSL.
2.  The parameter names match those of their Thrift counterparts, which would make converting to Thrift a little bit simpler.

Since `pystorm.Component.__init__` does next to nothing, this shouldn't slow anything down, and people are encouraged to override `Component.initialize()` when they want to setup connections to external data sources and that sort of thing.

Unless there are strong objections, I'm going to put together a PR for pystorm that adds these extra kwargs and get rid of the `Spec` stuff from the DSL.

@cdeil  I think this is completely appropriate for `photutils` -- performing photometry on PSF-matched images is a common use case.

When working with multiple bandpasses in UV/O/IR data with different PSF widths, the typical procedure is to blur all of the (n-1) images to the image with the widest (worst) PSF, which is usually the reddest band.  To do this, "matching" kernels are needed to go from the individual images to the reference image.  In your simple example of two images with sigma1 and sigma2, one wouldn't convolve image1 with sigma2 and convolve image2 with sigma1 (that works, but you're unnecessarily blurring too much).  If sigma2 > sigma1, then you can simply take image2 as the reference image and convolve image1 with a Gaussian of ``sigma = sqrt(sigma2**2 - sigma1**2)`` (that would be the "matching" kernel).  I think most people still use IRAF's [psfmatch](http://stsdas.stsci.edu/cgi-bin/gethelp.cgi?psfmatch) for this task, so a python replacement would be great!

Another benefit of the high-level class is integration with the ``SkyCoord`` class, which doesn't exist in the lower-level API.

In any case, having thought about this a bit more, I do agree that the fewer APIs we have to maintain, the better. One option therefore would be to also get rid of the low level API and keep *only* the high-level API. Just an idea!

> The readme instructions for Linux (not OSX because that does pip install nupic only) have to be updated each time the nupic.bindings version is bumped.

Actually, they don't. They need to be updated whenever there is a NuPIC release, because that is when the binary instructions are affected. Installing from binary only gets you the latest NuPIC version on pypi, which is tied to some nupic.bindings version, which may not necessarily be the latest one. We do not want to update the bindings version in the README because it may not be compatible with the latest NuPIC release.

This instruction in the README is actually [automatically updated by the release script we use](https://github.com/nupic-community/nupic.devtools/blob/master/bin/release_nupic#L314-L335) as a part of the release process. So it gets updated when it should be updated. 

And about your other ideas, I don't see the point in publishing a special pypi version for the latest, because pip knows that the latest version is the default, and it will prefer to install the latest if not given a version number. And for the "upstream" one... if people are installing from binary, they don't get the bleeding edge version of NuPIC, which is acceptable IMO. If they want the bleeding edge, they will need to compile themselves or wait for the next release, which should come pretty quickly.

Guys, you should not need to manually install `nupic.bindings` if you are building from source. The nupic build process will install it for you. So, hopefully all you need is to run `python setup.py install [--user]` from the nupic checkout directory. 

If you are installing from binary on Linux, you need to manually install the `nupic.bindings` binary like this:

    pip install https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/artifacts.numenta.org/numenta/nupic.core/releases/nupic.bindings/nupic.bindings-0.1.5-cp27-none-linux_x86_64.whl

Then you can `pip install nupic`. The reason you have to do this for Linux is [explained here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic#install-linux).

In principle this is what we want; however, consider the following example from Kinto:

```
$ git shortlog -sne | awk '{$1=""; sub(" ", ""); print}'
Rémy HUBSCHER <rhubscher@mozilla.com>
Mathieu Leplatre <mathieu@mozilla.com>
Alexis Métaireau <alexis@notmyidea.org>
Rémy HUBSCHER <hubscher.remy@gmail.com>
Alexis Metaireau <alexis@notmyidea.org>
Mathieu Leplatre <leplatrem@gmail.com>
Mathieu Agopian <mathieu@agopian.info>
Tarek Ziade <tarek@ziade.org>
dan phrawzty <phrawzty+github@gmail.com>
phrawzty <phrawzty+github@gmail.com>
Andy McKay <amckay@mozilla.com>
Fil <fil@rezo.net>
dan <phrawzty+github@gmail.com>
```

As you can see, some names are listed more than one (notably, I have terrible `.git/config` hygeine), which makes for a messy list. Git has a built-in mechanism for dealing with this ([mailmap](https://www.kernel.org/pub/software/scm/git/docs/git-shortlog.html)) but it requires knowledge of the contents of the shortlog ahead of time.

I propose that we process the list and remove duplicates on the fly by harnessing the **awesome power of `awk` and pipes**. :neckbeard: In the following example I pass the shortlog to `awk` (as above), then pipe it through two additional field sorts: one each for *name* and *email address*.

```
$ git shortlog -sne | awk '{$1=""; sub(" ", ""); print}' | awk -F'<' '!x[$1]++' | awk -F'<' '!x[$2]++'
Rémy HUBSCHER <rhubscher@mozilla.com>
Mathieu Leplatre <mathieu@mozilla.com>
Alexis Métaireau <alexis@notmyidea.org>
Mathieu Agopian <mathieu@agopian.info>
Tarek Ziade <tarek@ziade.org>
dan phrawzty <phrawzty+github@gmail.com>
Andy McKay <amckay@mozilla.com>
Fil <fil@rezo.net>
```

The release commit should be in a separate PR for many reasons in my eyes: 
- The bug fix or a feature request may fail and/or may need review amendments,  which is easier to do without release commits on top. *(tactical reason*)
- The maintainer can take advantage of the release to add other minor things, or say update I18N translations if applicable. Basically, it's up to the maintainer to decide about best time to release. *(strategical reason)*
- "Doing one thing at the time and doing it well."  Are we concentrating on making a nice bug fix or a nice feature implementation in the PR?  Or are we focusing on the release notes, the data migration procedures, the integration checks, etc? *(focus reason)*
- A PR in separate branch would make it easier to rebase on top of any other changes that may come before release. Or imagine if the release fails because Travis tag pushing fails  for the maintainer (happened a few times). It's not about making release days later, it could be simply 30 minutes later, after I18N phrases come. *(practical reason)*
- To make a release after every minor change asked by every service using the package would be definitely not a scalable model for the bright stable future. The lambda users would hate us because they cannot follow news stream, and PyPI.org sysadmins would hate us because we would be eating all their quotas. :)
   *(looking out for a stable future model; I know this case is different...)*
- Let's try to come with a scalable model, if a package is used by many services, because it wouldn't scale to make a new release after every commit. For example, one could imagine services relying on a pinned SHA1 from `master` branch, if they need to deploy quickly after a merge was done. These SHA1 never change, and the model would be more scalable with Ns services and Np patches?! *(looking out for a stable future model again)* 

*Disclaimer:* I was trying to muse about a scalable release model vs a scalable deployment model for the stable times in a generic use case when a package is used by Nu services,  contributed to by Nc people, and maintained by Nm people. I know that in this case things are different, the package is still alpha, and still not used beyond Zenodo... but perhaps it's time to come with scalable practices to pave the way for brighter tomorrows? Let's muse IRL...

tl;dr: in your buildozer.spec, add hostpython2 to the requirements option.

Yes, this is added by default. The only way to remove it is to edit the python-for-android source, as it's currently hardcoded in the AndroidManifest.xml file.

The reason it's there is that it used to be required by Kivy, but it hasn't been essential for some time. It's possible that Kivy assumes it's available and will fail if it can't write there, but that just needs a small fix in Kivy itself. I've been meaning to look at this for a while, but it looks like there wasn't a python-for-android issue open about it, so I've made one at https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/issues/1081 .

I'll close this issue as it's a python-for-android issue, but thanks for raising it.

I agree that there is a lot of similarity between the M2Crypto and pyLevelDB recipes setting the python linker and including the headers. Why is this not needed by _every_ python recipe is what I am wondering?
This part seems to be a bug btw, because without it the setup.py uses the default gcc for linking whereas the compilation step _does_ use the right gcc for the architecture.
```
# Set linker to use the correct gcc
        env['LDSHARED'] = env['CC'] 
```

Also these settings are recurring for all libraries that want to use the regular gnu c stl:
```
env['CFLAGS'] += ' -I' + self.ctx.ndk_dir + '/platforms/android-' + str(self.ctx.android_api) + '/arch-' + arch.arch.replace('eabi', '') + '/usr/include' + \
                         ' -I' + self.ctx.ndk_dir + '/sources/cxx-stl/gnu-libstdc++/' + self.ctx.toolchain_version + '/include' + \
                         ' -I' + self.ctx.ndk_dir + '/sources/cxx-stl/gnu-libstdc++/' + self.ctx.toolchain_version + '/libs/' + arch.arch + '/include'
        env['CXXFLAGS'] = env['CFLAGS']
        env['CXXFLAGS'] += ' -frtti'
        env['CXXFLAGS'] += ' -fexceptions'
        env['LDFLAGS'] += ' -L' + self.ctx.ndk_dir + '/sources/cxx-stl/gnu-libstdc++/' + self.ctx.toolchain_version + '/libs/' + arch.arch + \
                          ' -lgnustl_shared'
```
Perhaps a special recipe class could be made for that as well?

Here is a preliminary list of functions that are going to be implemented in `datatable`:

Exposed function (Python):

- `aggregate(df, options)`
  - Input: 
    - `df`: frame to aggregate
    - `options`: options to control the aggregation, including number of bins, radius, minimum number of rows to aggregate, maximum number of dimensions, seed, etc.
  - Output:
    - `df_aggregated`: dataframe consisting of row_id, x_group_id, y_group_id, etc, where row_id's are the original row_id's from df and the group_id's are the bin id's for the aggregated dimensions.
  - Implementation: do aggregation based on the dataframe and options supplied by invoking one of the following internal methods for different cases:
    - `aggregate1DCategorical(df, options)`
    - `aggregate2DCategorical(df, options)`
    - `aggregate1DContinuous(df, options)`
    - `aggregate2DContinuous(df, options)`
    - `aggregate2DMixed(df, options)`
    - `aggregateND(df, options)`

Internal functions (C++):
- `aggregate1DCategorical(df, options)` 
   - Implementation: uniform 1D binning for a categorical column.
- `aggregate2DCategorical(df, options)` 
   - Implementation: uniform 2D binning for two categorical columns.
- `aggregate1DContinuous(df, options)` 
  - Implementation: uniform 1D binning for a continuous column.
- `aggregate2DContinuous(df, options)` 
  - Implementation: uniform 2D binning for two continuous columns.
- `aggregate2DMixed(df, options)` 
  - Implementation: uniform 2D binning for two columns, one continuous and one categorical.
- `aggregateND(df, options)` 
  - Implementation: for the general case do ND binning using Leland's algorithm, see https://www.cs.uic.edu/~wilkinson/Publications/outliers.pdf for more details.

> One advantage of NFF (one file per column) is that columns can be added and deleted very easily and robustly.

This kind of model is even used in databases and is called 6NF (6th normal form). Ability to add/delete columns without altering table object is called _non destructive schema evolution_ and is crucial feature of Anchor Modeling (6NF design technique). A quote from wikipedia:
> Anchor modeling is an agile database modeling technique suited for information that changes over time both in structure and content.

When switching to single file model we are losing this ability.
@stephlocke is expert on that :)

---

I also agree that thousands of columns are not that common cases in SQL RDBMS, from my experience in big warehouses I was having 200-1000 columns. When having more then better to melt your data and transform columns into rows to process by group.
I can imaging there are use cases in ML where you need to have as _flat_ data as possible, like _one-hot encoding_. This justify to implement efficient backend for big amounts of columns, but at the same time I don't think it make sense to drop existing __"no format"__ model.

---

Before making decisions it will be best to see benchmark of both.

Here's the data on screen altitudes and wind velocities that Steve Kent collected for our hack. I selected 3 altitudes out of this list as nominal suggested values in the docstring. Note there is no correlated data on `r0` values (or equivalently `C_n^2` profiles) as a function of altitude.

Note the wind velocities below are the same for 0-2.5km altitudes. 

The Global Data Assimilation System (GDAS), run by the NOAA National Center for Environmental Prediction (NCEP), puts out various datasets that might be of interest to LSST. GDAS produces "analyses" every six hours, giving a large number of parameters tabulated at 24 altitudes in the atmosphere covering the entire Earth. The following is an example of wind velocities extracted from a file for 2014, Feb 13, 6h UT at the longitude and latitude of CTIO. (Wind velocities are m/s). Altitudes are
approximate.
