I think it's fine to implement this as a photon shooting extension as Craig has done so far.  

Then when using photon shooting for the main image rendering, the photons have their exact positions, which are then propagated into the silicon.  When we want to do FFT rendering, we can do that onto an image the normal way (possibly with smaller pixel scale than what we eventually want), build an interpolated image out of it and photon shoot that through the same module.  I'm pretty sure I know how to write those steps in the python layer.  So it's fine for Craig to focus on getting this working via drawShoot.

Doing this all chromatically is a different issue.  We don't have any chromatic stuff for photon shooting currently.  But even when we do that, I still think we'll want this to be a photon shooting module that we will run from FFT-drawn images if that's how we want to do the image rendering.

Hi @disco-stu,

you are running very old patroni version: 1.1.

Those days Patroni wasn't retrying failing requests to consul cluster.
Please upgrade to 1.3.3 it should help.

If consul is not accessible or not healthy, Patroni will try to re-execute the same request multiple times during `retry_timeout` (default value is 10 seconds). If after 10 seconds request is still failing Patroni will restart master in read-only.

So, here's the underlying issue: if "Listen" is set to anything other than "0.0.0.0", which maps to "*" in postgresql.conf, then initialization will fail.

Given this, maybe it shouldn't be a configurable parameter?

Hi Jeff, this is the first time that issue has come up from external to Rally/CA.  So I salute you as being external adventurer #1 with respect to pyral automated testing.  The brief response is that you'll need to set up a rally_targets.py file in the test subdirectory and populate it with creds, workspaces and projects that you have access to.  Next, you'd probably have to alter some tests to reflect data in your system.  You could easily change all the YETI_* items to your favorite name, cola, car manufacturer, galaxy, etc.  and you'd have to set up an API_KEY or two for your username.
The longer term would be to modify the testing to allow for dynamic data creation to eliminate the need for static data.  However, as this is _still_ a hackathon project (that is nearly 7 years old...) that is officially unsupported, I don't see it as high on probability list any time soon.

TLDR;  specify your fetch fields as a list of Attributes, eg. fetch="Name,FormattedID,etc"

If you want to get at an entity's attribute sub-attribute (like the case here where you want to get the FormattedID of the Story's PortfolioItem attribute), you'll need to provide a fetch list (a string of comma separated Attribute ElementNames (ie., no spaces in the Attribute name and no space after each comma) including the Attribute ElementName for any Attribute sub-attribute you intend to reference.  In your example code, you have a fetch=True which will allow you to access each Attribute for the entity type you specify (ie., 'Story') but not for sub-objects (like PortfolioItem).  

There's indeed a problem with the details() method that will be fixed in v1.2.4. 

For requests that result in a large set of items, you are better off specifying all the Attributes you will be referencing as opposed to using fetch=True, especially if you end up using the details() method. 
The reason for that is when you request Story items with fetch=True and you use the details() method, it results in a separate request for the PortfolioItem (which does a fetch=True there also)  (let alone the cost of assembling _every_ field for every item on the server side as well as deconstructing it on the pyral side).  So instead of one or a few requests, you end up with essentially "number of qualifying items" requests which can get visibly tiresome looking at the staccato output on the console or waiting for the process to complete.

You will have to do that for any packages with C extensions since you need to compile them on a system that is compatible with lambda. I see in your screenshot you have one .whl file `PyYAML-3.12-cp36-cp36m-win_amd64.whl` that won't work on lambdas the suffix `win_amd64` implies this means it was compiled on windows. 

The error you got initially is because Chalice can't package the dependencies for AWS Lambda from your current machine because there are C dependencies that need compiling, and the maintainers of that dependency did not add wheel files for `manylinux1` or `linux_x86` both of which work on lambda.

So you have to do the work instead of the maintainers which means you need to get an EC2 instance spun up that is Amazon Linux (the same as what Lambda runs on) and compile those dependencies on that box. That will generate a wheel file that is compatible with Lambda.

The probability of the`ProbabilisticIntentParser` is not affected by the fact that the other parser is enabled or not.
The feedback we get about probabilities is that they are usually lower than what people expect. For now there is not much we can do about it if we stick with the current model (logistic regression) which performs quite well in terms of accuracy. In theory, you shouldn't have to worry about the absolute probabilities, as the intent which is returned is supposed to be the most likely one. However, comparing probabilities among the top intents could be useful but for now only the most likely intent is returned. See the thread about this: https://github.com/snipsco/snips-nlu/issues/623.

You shouldn't have to use entities if you don't need to, but that can be faster indeed to generate entity values with the intent vocabulary (if you know it exhaustively), instead of generating multiple sentences trying to cover all the vocabulary. So I guess that's something to try.

Intent classification relies on characteristic words so you can also try to add more sentences and have them contain some domain specific words.

I like the concept of scoring, but fear that scores that are too general may lead to meaningless comparison between reconstruction, or misguided curation efforts (e.g. curating to achieve a high score, rather than curating to make the reconstruction as predictive/representative as possible for the intended purpose).

To illustrate the point, let's consider some of the "easy to score" tests that @ChristianLieven brought up (sorry to pick on them, I realize these were just quick examples):

> The fraction of blocked reactions (in rich medium) should be low.

While unblocking these reactions might improve performance, and removing them may reduce the size of the reconstruction, penalizing their inclusion may harm the use of the reconstruction as a knowledgebase. For example, if a particular understudied organism has a pathway that is blocked because it contains a novel, unidentified reaction that connects it to the rest of the network, the reconstruction's score might be penalized for including this blocked pathway, even though identification/characterization of the blocked portions may represent important biological knowledge. If the goal is to improve such a reconstruction in an iterative fashion, I don't think that having such a metric contribute to the overall score for an organism will encourage that.

> The fraction of reactions without GPR should be low.

Similar to the logic above, this might discourage authors from including reactions for which there is substantial experimental evidence, yet no known gene.


I think the best compromise is the suggestion that @beatrizgj made, e.g. there should be category-specific scores. I favor leaving an overall score out entirely, although I realize that makes it more difficult to communicate the overall quality of the reconstruction. Maybe presenting only the results of the 'Hard' tests as the overall score might work better (e.g. if there's a mass balance issue, I can't think of any way that penalizing the overall score would hurt the science).

I also particularly like the idea of known failures @jonovik . Framing some of the test results like that could guide/prioritize future curation efforts in a way that I think is more constructive than reporting a continuous score.

如楼上所说，电脑版的ss已经用socks5做了远程dns解析，但是电脑端没办法先通过远程服务器解析dns，然后再根据结果选择是否走代理。我猜你说的应该是这个问题吧。

现在电脑端的代理选择是靠域名和本地电脑的dns解析的ip来选择是否走代理的，而安卓端是先通过远程服务器解析dns，再根据返回的ip来决定是否走代理。这是两者的区别。所以电脑端一般通过域名来决定是否走代理，因为国内的dns解析的ip是不可靠的。就效果来说当然是安卓端的要好一些。那为什么电脑端没有采用安卓端的方案呢？这就要提到安卓端的实现原理了。

* 安卓端用了ss-libev的代码，ss-libev本身就自带一个转发dns的ss-tunel，而电脑端一开始就没有设计这个功能。

* 安卓端用iptables（在root的情况下）和vpn劫持了所有的ip包，然后再通过tunsocks把ip包转换为socks5，最终实现了一个类似透明代理的效果。所以它可以先通过远程服务器解析dns，再根据结果选择是否走代理。而电脑端没有实现透明代理的功能（ss-libev的电脑端有这个功能，但需要配合linux系统的iptables才能用），它只是通过设置系统代理来转发数据。至于选择是否走代理，只能通过系统自带的pac功能来实现。如果电脑端能劫持系统的所有数据包（或是只劫持dns包也可以），比如用vpn或其他手段实现一个透明代理，那么就可以像安卓端那样远程解析dns。


就目前的状况来说，我觉得实现这个功能还是非常有必要的！而实际上，我最近一直都在研究怎么在windows/linux系统上实现一个简单易用的透明代理，或许不久的将来会实现吧。

@jsirois : One thing I hadn't thought of (and which you may now have thought of): we currently don't account for equality of `Node::Output` types... a `TaskNode` outputs `core::Value` instances, which we do not currently implement equality (in the form of rust's `Eq`) for. In places where we need both `Eq` and `Hash` we convert to a `core::Key`, which memoizes the `core::Value` to assign it a unique id... at least in the case of putting a python value in a hashtable, I think the Key abstraction is worthwhile, because we avoid a very large number of calls into python.

I think that in the case of un-dirtying a `Node`, you would only need equality (and not hash)... maybe in that case adding an explicit `externs::equals(Value, Value)` would be worthwhile, and cheaper than converting into `Key`s.

I have also struggled with this. In essence you need to set up some channel backend, test database, start daphne (ASGI) server and some workers, run the behave tests and then tear everything down afterwards.

I looked into the channels runserver command and with what I learned from that created this behave example (not behave-django but hopefully it will be a useful basis/discussion point). This works for me with:

```bash
$ behave
```

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
# environment.py for behave and Django with Channels...
import os
import sys
import django
import threading
from copy import copy
from splinter import Browser
from django.conf import settings
from django.test.runner import DiscoverRunner
from django.core.management import call_command
from daphne.server import Server, build_endpoint_description_strings
from channels import DEFAULT_CHANNEL_LAYER, channel_layers
from channels.staticfiles import StaticFilesConsumer
from channels.worker import Worker

# Setup the environment for standalone
pth = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
sys.path.append(pth)
os.environ.setdefault("DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", "myapp.settings")
django.setup()


TEST_HOST = '192.168.1.179'  # You'll probably be 127.0.0.1
TEST_PORT = 5000 # Whatever you like, trying to avoid a clash with dev server on 8000
TEST_CHANNEL_LAYER = 'TEST_CHANNEL_LAYER'

# Using a copy of default. WARNING: if devserver is running will clash with its use of the same backend
test_channel_config = copy(settings.CHANNEL_LAYERS[DEFAULT_CHANNEL_LAYER])
# Changes to the config here...
# Set up our own channel layer using a copy of the default one so we don't name clash with any running devserver
settings.CHANNEL_LAYERS[TEST_CHANNEL_LAYER] = test_channel_config


class WorkerThread(threading.Thread):
    """
    Class that runs a channel worker
    """
    def __init__(self, channel_layer):
        super(WorkerThread, self).__init__()
        self.channel_layer = channel_layer

    def run(self):
        worker = Worker(channel_layer=self.channel_layer, signal_handlers=False)
        worker.ready()
        worker.run()


def after_scenario(context, scenario):
    """
   Clear after every scenario 
    """
    call_command('flush', interactive=False, verbosity=0)

def before_all(context):

    # Use a runner to setup and teardown database instances for us
    context.runner = DiscoverRunner()
    context.runner.setup_test_environment()
    context.old_config = old_config = context.runner.setup_databases()

    # Set up a named channel for our workers to listen on and have them serve static files also
    channel_layer = channel_layers[TEST_CHANNEL_LAYER]
    channel_layer.router.check_default(http_consumer = StaticFilesConsumer())

    # Fire off some workers 
    for _ in range(4):
        worker = WorkerThread(channel_layer)
        worker.daemon = True
        worker.start()

    # build the endpoint description string from host/port options
    endpoints = build_endpoint_description_strings(host=TEST_HOST, port=TEST_PORT)

    # Start the daphne server
    context.server = Server(
        channel_layer=channel_layer,
        endpoints=endpoints,
        http_timeout=60,
        verbosity=0
    )

    server_process = threading.Thread(target=context.server.run)
    server_process.daemon = True
    server_process.start()

    # Now the web driver we will need
    context.base_url = 'http://{}:{}'.format(TEST_HOST, TEST_PORT)
    context.browser = Browser('chrome', **executable_path)

def after_all(context):
    # Quit our browser
    context.browser.quit()
    context.browser = None

    # Note: Server and worker threads are daemonized so they die at the end of the script

    # Teardown the test database and environment
    context.runner.teardown_databases(context.old_config)
    context.runner.teardown_test_environment()

```

I hope it helps someone.

Sorry, testing with `pip install -e .` is an old habbit based on the assumption that it invokes `setup.py` which installs dependencies. This doesn't hold true right now because dependencies have been entirely removed from `setup.py` and are only downloaded with `make assets`.

Just doing `pip install -r requirements.txt` will not help because `kolibri/dist` may be unclean and thus overwriting any of these.

I've redone the manual test with `make assets` and can confirm that nothing seems broken.

Since this is intended as a no-op, and this test affirms that, I consider it safe for 0.7.

Looking at [just the English](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/6/stopwords-json/master/dist/en.json), it's too aggressive to filter them out completely.

Some examples:

* `example`
* `greetings`
* `second`
*  `x`, `y`, and `z`

Instead, we can split the query up into `all_words`, `critical_words`, and `stop_words`. Then the ranked queries might look something like:

1. exact match in title
2. all words in title
3. all critical words in title
4. exact match in description
5. all words in description
6. all critical words in description
7. any critical word in title, reverse-sorted by word length
8. any stop word in title, reverse-sorted by word length

@benjaoming Executing vacuum in Postgres takes a long, long time. Autovacuum in postgresql does not delay the server normal work so, setting it up in the server config is the best thing to do.

However, in my experience, autovacuum is not enough in postgresql. After some time the db grows too much and becomes slow, but in this case vacuum must be executed periodically (every week in servers with a high load, around 1 million inserts by day, or monthly with fewer inserts). 

In my opinion, we should cross that bridge when we come to it. I don't think we could do it in a standard method. Depending on the server, different intervals and execution times (nightly if possible) should be considered.

Also, we could think of executing it after kolibri stops, but bearing in mind that vacuum might take easily 30 minutes or more, depending on the size of the database.

Essentially, 0.99999999 contains most of the API breakage that's needed to reach a point where there's a sane, stable API for 1.0 (and I think I can genuinely say we're actually, finally, realistically close…); some of which was needed to fix long-standing bugs and others that we decided to ship in the same release to just have a single breaking release rather than many. As with many projects, while we've been 0.y we've never guaranteed API stability (though it has indeed been a while since we last broke anything).

`rsa_public_key.json` follows JWK format as defined in [RFC7517](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7517).

python-jwt currently could read JWKs of RSA or octet and construct JWK objects from their content.  So you can convert your PEM to JWK manually, then pass it to python-jwk.

Furthermore python-jwt could also read a PEM-encoded RSA key and convert it to JSON-serializable Python dict as below.

```python
from jwt import jwk_from_pem

with open('YOUR-RSA-KEY.pem', 'rb') as fh:
    jwk = jwk_from_pem(fh.read())

jwk.to_dict()  # => {'kty': 'RSA', ...}
```

Dear @maelp , as I understand, you are interested to look at the room impulse response of the room. There is a simpler way to do this. When you run the `shoebox.simulate()` line above, all room impulse responses will be computed and stored in `shoebox.rirs`. Then, the impulse response between the `m`-th microphone and the `s`-th source can be accessed at `shoebox.rirs[m][s]`.

In case you are only interested in getting the impulse response (as opposed to simulate the propagation of a particular sound sample), you can omit the source signal specification and call `shoebox.compute_rir()` instead of `simulate()`. Here is the modified example.
```
'''
A simple example of using pyroomacoustics to compute
the room impulse response between one source and one microphone.
'''

import numpy as np
import pyroomacoustics as pra
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# room dimension
room_dim = [5, 4, 6]

# Create the shoebox
shoebox = pra.ShoeBox(
    room_dim,
    absorption=0.2,
    fs=fs,
    max_order=15,
    )

# source and mic locations
shoebox.add_source([2, 3.1, 2])
shoebox.add_microphone_array(
        pra.MicrophoneArray(
            np.array([[2, 1.5, 2]]).T, 
            shoebox.fs)
        )

# run ism
shoebox.compute_rir()

# this plots the RIR between the 1st source and the 1st microphone
rir_time = np.arange(len(shoebox.rir[0][0])) / shoebox.fs
plt.plot(rir_time, shoebox.rir[0][0])
```

Yeah, this happened and I had to add the exception manually to the security group. Quoting an AWS team member:

> Hey Guido,
>
> I was just cruising through the Deis source code, specifically contrib/ec2/deis.template.json, that essentially created the infrastructure your Deis cluster is running on (I’m assuming you followed the Installing Deis on AWS guide on Deis website).
>
>This bit in that file defines the AWS security group your balancer belongs to:
>
>"DeisWebELBSecurityGroup": {
>     "Type": "AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup",
>     "Properties": {
>       "GroupDescription": "Deis Web ELB SecurityGroup",
>       "SecurityGroupIngress": [
>         {"IpProtocol": "tcp", "FromPort": "80", "ToPort": "80", "CidrIp": "0.0.0.0/0"},
>         {"IpProtocol": "tcp", "FromPort": "2222", "ToPort": "2222", "CidrIp": "0.0.0.0/0"}
>       ],
>       "VpcId": { "Ref" : "VPC" }
>     }
>   },
>
>Notice the absence of 443?
>
>The Deis guys were correct in telling you that doing SSL termination on the ELB is a best practice, they may have forgotten to tell you that you would need to add inbound 443 to the ELB’s security group. It’s not that ELB isn’t serving your cert, its that the security group is preventing inbound 443 from the internet to your ELB because you are not explicitly allowing it.  I would really recommend you make this change in your source code too, but you can modify the security group in-place. Find the elastic load balancer in question in the AWS EC2 console, look on the details tab for “Source Security Group” and grab the security group name. Then click on the Security Group section on the left bar and filter by that source security group name. Go to the inbound tab for that security group, click the edit button, and add HTTPS over 443 for 0.0.0.0/0. You should be good to go.

3. If both points above are justified - then that would be an issue for Anaconda.

Pavol Juhas wrote:
>
> Here is a patch that seems to fix the |conda install --revision| bug 
> for conda 4.3.24.
> The |dists| variable is looped over twice in the 
> |ensure_linked_actions|, but if it were a generator the second loop is 
> empty. This fix turns it to a tuple which iterates in the same way 
> every time.
>
> diff --git a/conda/plan.py b/conda/plan.py
> index 306eaf61..b5bdb08b 100644
> --- a/conda/plan.py
> +++ b/conda/plan.py
> @@ -805,7 +805,7 @@  def revert_actions(prefix, revision=-1, index=None):
>       if state == curr:
>           return {}
>
> -     dists = (Dist(s) for s in state)
> +     dists = tuple(Dist(s) for s in state)
>       actions = ensure_linked_actions(dists, prefix)
>       for dist in curr - state:
>           add_unlink(actions, Dist(dist))
>

@kalefranz the problem here is that `misc.clone_env` doesn't handle symbolic links properly. If the environment has a symbolic link, it copies the file into the new environment instead of creating a duplicate symbolic link. So then when attempting to do `shutil.copystat(src, dst)`, it's trying to copy the flags from a symbolic link onto an actual file.

In this case, `libgcc_s` is an absolute symbolic link to a system library, so we might as well duplicate the link.

@FOR /F "delims=" %%i IN ('@CALL "%_CONDA_EXE%" shell.cmd.exe activate %*') DO @SET "_TEMP_SCRIPT_PATH=%%i"

> Isn't the whole issue here a reproducible case for exactly that? I mean apart from the off-topic discussion about the style of the version numbers which I moved to a separate issue.

I don't see it that way, no. `conda` has never been "silently ignoring" anything. And the issue is not because it's "non-parseable". You can verify for yourself that the `VersionOrder` code has not changed in a _long_ time (though it has been moved into its own module), and it has never had difficulty parsing your custom version numbers. I don't fully understand why your particular behavior changed between `4.0.2` and `4.0.4`, but `VersionOrder` itself is not to blame.

> To me it seems obvious that the libgd *.bioconda dependency description I used (see the linked PR above) cannot be parsed anymore.

If it could not parse it, it would throw an exception. But what we know is that it is interpreting your `bioconda` versions as *older* than the ones in `default`. If you want to restore functionality for now, you follow the advice offered in the other thread and add `post`.

You can get 4.5.1 via

    conda config --add channels conda-canary
    conda update conda

I believe we now have this solved.  I've also seen places where I've reproduced this type of behavior with older versions of conda, and it's fixed in newer versions of conda.  I'm going to close this issue.  If anyway disagrees, we can reopen it.

Sounds like you may want to consider building packages for your particular needs. Every package has the conda-build recipe embedded within it, which would be the best starting point.

@pvanderlinden `conda config --set auto_update_conda False` might not exist in your version of miniconda or whatever you are using to install conda- I got this workaround to work by using Miniconda 3.9.1 to install conda version 4.2.13 and then set no update (otherwise any further installs in the root conda env will try and update conda too).

```RUN cd /tmp && \
    mkdir -p $CONDA_DIR && \
    wget --quiet https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda3-3.9.1-Linux-x86_64.sh && \
    echo "6c6b44acdd0bc4229377ee10d52c8ac6160c336d9cdd669db7371aa9344e1ac3 *Miniconda3-3.9.1-Linux-x86_64.sh" | sha256sum -c - && \
    /bin/bash Miniconda3-3.9.1-Linux-x86_64.sh -f -b -p $CONDA_DIR && \
    rm Miniconda3-3.9.1-Linux-x86_64.sh && \
    $CONDA_DIR/bin/conda install --yes conda=4.2.13 && \
    conda config --set auto_update_conda False```

Here is a regexp that finds the verbose and level formats of VUnit and the individual fields of these.

``` python
custom_levels = [] # Allows configuration of regexp
standard_levels = ['FAILURE', 'ERROR', 'WARNING', 'INFO', 'DEBUG', 'VERBOSE']
time_units = ['fs' ,'ps', 'ns', 'us' ,'ms', 'sec', 'min', 'hr']
vunit_log_pattern = re.compile(
    # A time specification is available in the verbose format but not in the level format
    '(?P<time_spec>' +
        '(?P<time>\d+ (%s))' % '|'.join(time_units) +
        ': ' +
    ')?' +
    '(?P<level>%s)' % '|'.join(standard_levels + custom_levels) + # Standard or custom level
    # A location specification is optional in the verbose format and has a log source ID and/or a file location
    '(?P<location_spec> in ' +
        '(?P<src>[^:(]+)?' +
        r'(\((?P<file_name>[^:]+):(?P<line_num>\d+)\))?' +
    ')?' +
    ': ' +
    '(?P<msg>.*)') # The message is the rest of the line
```

It will also find failing VHDL asserts from Aldec's simulators since their format is identical to the VUnit level format.

Adding a regexp like this will cover most other interesting outputs

``` python
general_pattern = re.compile(r'(?P<level>fatal|failure|error|warning)[^a-zA-Z]',re.IGNORECASE)
```

but there is a need for simulator specific patterns to do really good. For example

``` python
ghdl_compile_error_pattern = re.compile(r'Failed to compile');
```

@burtonator Shard splitting doesn't work because document<->shard mapping is handled by something like this (we teach this in Core ES training):

```
hash(_id) % shard_count
```

Shard count has to be static for this to work.  If you _could_ split shards, you'd ruin the math there and not be able to find documents _by `_id`_ in that index again.   Yes, if you anticipate needing that many shards, starting with that number makes sense.  However, there are, as mentioned, trade-offs.  There are reasons to have more shards, like if your index is so big that the shards would be bigger than 50G (the maximum _recommended_ size).  

Elasticity can be increased by using aliases.  You can re-index into another index with more shards, if needed, and use aliases to point from A->B in an atomic API call, keeping your data available constantly.

If you have a 5G heap, I really don't recommend using 20+1 shards per index unless you only have a few indices.  Those recommendations I left are best case scenarios.  Elasticsearch will _squash_ those desired amounts to work with whatever it can get.  I've seen the 250M for active shards compressed down to 47M, and inactive shards down to 760K.  It will eventually reach the point where it simply cannot allocate any more shards, however, and it will fail to index any more data.  It will do this silently, and without warning.  Search will still work, however.

Out of curiosity, why would you want shard splitting?  What advantage does 20 shards give over 10?  More shards, as mentioned, increases the number of places a node has to look for the data it wants.  An Elasticsearch user I recently did some troubleshooting with at a meetup did something similar.  They counted the number of threads in their system and thought that more shards would more effectively use CPU cores.  They were doing over 1,000,000 queries per second on their 20+ node cluster, but it was beginning to have outages when certain queries would happen.  When it came out that they had 16+1 shards across some 300 indices, my colleague and I immediately suggested they take those indices down to 2+2 (or 3) shards for the < 10G indices, and 3+2 for the 10G > 20G indices, and a similar pattern for other indices.  More replicas means faster searches, more shards means faster indexing.

My documentation sources cannot be shared as it is from the Core ES training slides (which are only available to training attendees) and the Knowledge Base we share with our support customers.  The memory limits are especially mentioned in time-series indexing cases, because the index count always grows.  If you don't plan on having continuous index growth, you would likely never hit those limits. 

If you are planning on having such a big cluster, I recommend sending devs to our Core ES training and engineers to our soon-to-be-released ops training.  These concepts, and more, are taught in these courses.  We also discuss cluster design in support kick-off calls.

> 
```
timestring: '%Y.%m.%d.%H'
```
Does not match
```
logstash-2018.08.07
```
This is why you get an empty list.

Try dropping the `.%H` off, and change the `units`:
```
timestring: '%Y.%m.%d'
units: days
```
This will still not match `logstash-2018.08.07`, but it will match `logstash-2018.08.06` and older.

Truthfully, I don't think I can safely remove this functionality, as I look at it.  While you correctly read that the check for `/_snapshot/_status` is to ensure no other snapshot is running, it's not limited to the current repository.  Elasticsearch might have multiple repositories configured, but only one snapshot can be run at any given time, to any of them.  That's why Curator checks for snapshots across _all_ possible repositories.  

While I _could_ theoretically test my way around this by just not testing for existing snapshots and try to catch failures with a `try/except` block, it's a monkey patch approach to a problem that doesn't exist anywhere outside of AWS ES.  As such, I'm not going to be fixing this.  I apologize for the inconvenience.

This is a usage issue and not a bug.  All future comments will go to the [Discuss forum topic](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/failed-to-delete-indices-when-curator-configured-for-multiple-hosts-in-same-cluster/109373) I just discovered you created.

Hi @rlewkowicz, in the help text for VM create:

```
    --public-ip-address                : Name of the public IP address when creating one (default)
                                         or referencing an existing one. Can also reference an
                                         existing public IP by ID or specify "" for None.
```

The default behavior is to create a new (dynamic) public IP, which is why the backend VM specifies `--public-ip-address ""`.  In your case, you would specify the name (if it is in the same resource group) or ID (if it is in a different resource group) of your existing public IP.  If you wanted to create the public IP as a static IP instead of dynamic, you would specify `--public-ip-address-allocation static`.

this is clearly an aiohttp bug.  This is a re-manifestation of https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1954.  Based on the current impl the first request will timeout, at which point all the other requests will timeout waiting to get a connector from the pool.   I highly recommend re-evaluating how aiohttp's timeouts work.  I still firmly believe it doesn't make sense, timeouts should mean time waiting for a response from the server, not waiting from the time the request was called from the client.  This is a fundamental problem with out timeouts work in aiohttp.  This issue will continue popping up in the future as various client methods are called between `request` and the return to request.  For example, if tracing call stalls for too long, guess what, your `get` is going to throw a timeout error...etc etc

Could you explain how `Scheduler.close` and `job.close` Works?

```py
# scheduler.close
    async def close(self):
        if self._closed:
            return
        self._closed = True  # prevent adding new jobs

        jobs = self._jobs
        if jobs:
            # cleanup pending queue
            # all job will be started on closing
            while not self._pending.empty():
                self._pending.get_nowait()
            await asyncio.gather(
                *[job._close(self._close_timeout) for job in jobs],
                loop=self._loop, return_exceptions=True)
            self._jobs.clear()
        self._failed_tasks.put_nowait(None)
        await self._failed_task
```

Here is my guess for how `scheduler.close` works :
1. first empty the pending queue, which means jobs in pending queue won't be executed.
2. then it waits for the jobs to close with a close timeout.
3. finally it processes the exception raised by failed tasks


```py
# job._close
    async def _close(self, timeout):
        if self._closed:
            return
        self._closed = True
        if self._task is None:
            # the task is closed immediately without actual execution
            # it prevents a warning like
            # RuntimeWarning: coroutine 'coro' was never awaited
            self._start()
        if not self._task.done():
            self._task.cancel()
        # self._scheduler is None after _done_callback()
        scheduler = self._scheduler
        try:
            with async_timeout.timeout(timeout=timeout,
                                       loop=self._loop):
                await self._task
        except asyncio.CancelledError:
            pass
        except asyncio.TimeoutError as exc:
            if self._explicit:
                raise
            context = {'message': "Job closing timed out",
                       'job': self,
                       'exception': exc}
            if self._source_traceback is not None:
                context['source_traceback'] = self._source_traceback
            scheduler.call_exception_handler(context)
        except Exception as exc:
            if self._explicit:
                raise

            self._report_exception(exc)
```
And my guess for how `job.close` works:

It starts the job and then cancels it immediately, and then waits for it to cancel within the timeout, if the job is not canceled within the timeout, it raises the exception and reports it.

So if my guess is correct, in order to use  `scheduler.close()`,  I need to handle the `CancelError` myself, and set a larger timeout?

Ok, what do you think about this idea:

1. Instructor creates a GPG key on the system. The public key for this gets copied to the exchange the first time something is released. Assignments that are released are released normally (not encrypted).
2. The first time students try to access the exchange, nbgrader imports the public key.
3. When students submit something, nbgrader automatically imports the public key and encrypts their submission to that key.
4. The `inbound` directory is readable by everyone. Encrypted submissions get copied to the inbound directory in the same way that they are currently, except that permissions are set such that they cannot be modified/removed by other students. So, other students can see who has submitted stuff, but can't actually see the contents of the submission.
5. When collecting submissions, nbgrader will automatically decrypt them using the secret key that only the instructor has.

Let's not exaggerate the dangers - a naive user running the notebook as root on their local machine is not exposing anything. And if they do expose anything, either because other people can log on to the machine and connect to the server, or because they configure it to serve on the public network interface, it's pretty bad to run the server as their own user anyway (see [relevant xkcd](https://xkcd.com/1200/)).

However, it's never a good idea to run with more privileges than necessary, and I don't think many people really want to run the server as root, so :+1: to adding a confirmation flag.

`flit install` also has a similar check, and requires an environment variable `FLIT_ROOT_INSTALL` to override it.

- we have a company-wide installation of Jupyter & JupyterHub with dockerspawner

- `Users/` is mounted as read-only in JupyterHub
- users can only edit their own notebooks in `Home/` which is a read/write mount pointing to `Users/<username>/`

Unfortunately in this setup, other users are unable to make copies of each other's notebooks from the read-only `Users/` tree into their read/write `Home/` tree, because Jupyter's `Make a copy...` function only creates a copy in the same folder as the original.

Here's the relevant part from our JupyterHub configuration:
```python
c.DockerSpawner.volumes = {

```

I showed in OCA sprint to @sbidoul How to auto bump version in python package with a release/tag after merge.
(I know here the matter is not auto-bump version)

The point is that working with python package you don't change the version in each PR.

Imagine 3 developers working in a PR in the same module.

| Module           | Developer      | Stable version     | Bumped version |  Status of PR |
|-----------------|---------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Module A | Tom  | 8.0.1.0.0 | 8.0.1.0.1 | Open |
| Module A |        Jhon       |    8.0.1.0.0          | 8.0.1.0.1 | Open |
| Module A |        Jarry       |         8.0.1.0.0     | 8.0.1.0.1 | Open | 

After days of feedback and more commits a PR is merged.

| Module           | Developer      | Stable version     | Bumped version |  Status of PR |
|-----------------|---------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Module A | Tom  | 8.0.1.0.0 | 8.0.1.0.1 | Merged |
| Module A |        Jhon       |    8.0.1.0.0          | 8.0.1.0.1 | Conflicts manifest file |
| Module A |        Jarry       |         8.0.1.0.0     | 8.0.1.0.1 | Conflicts manifest file |

After fix conflicts

| Module           | Developer      | Stable version     | Bumped version |  Status of PR |
|-----------------|---------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Module A |        Jhon       |    8.0.1.0.1          | 8.0.1.0.2 | Open (Rebased) |
| Module A |        Jarry       |         8.0.1.0.1     | 8.0.1.0.2 | Open (Rebased) |

After days of feedback and more commits a PR is merged.

| Module           | Developer      | Stable version     | Bumped version |  Status of PR |
|-----------------|---------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Module A |        Jhon       |    8.0.1.0.1          | 8.0.1.0.2 | Conflicts manifest file |
| Module A |        Jarry       |         8.0.1.0.1     | 8.0.1.0.2 | Merged |

After fix conflicts (Again)

| Module           | Developer      | Stable version     | Bumped version |  Status of PR |
|-----------------|---------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Module A |        Jhon       |    8.0.1.0.2         | 8.0.1.0.3 | Open (Rebased*2) |

IMHO a bump version is not work of a pull request.
If a process increase the conflicts of a PR create a game that discourage new contributions.

I my case if I see a opened PR of the same module... better I will wait a merge before of create my PR today  in order to avoid the game of conflicts, fix, rebase, conflicts, fix, rebase...

IMHO It should be work of the maintainer who decide what PR merge first.
But this is a work that the maintainer don't want to do.

For pylint-odoo we are using [pbr](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pbr/) and if we create a new `git tag -s v8.0.1.0.2 {SHA}` then a [release](https://github.com/OCA/pylint-odoo/releases) is auto-created and a new version is auto-deployed in pypi.

We decide the version with the tag.

Other tools to bumpversion is [bumpversion](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/bumpversion) to auto increase the version `{major.minor.patch}` use the following commands:
  - For increase .patch section
     -  `bumpversion patch --tag`
  - For increase .minor section
     -  `bumpversion minor --tag`
  - For increase .major section
     - `bumpversion major --tag`

This auto-create a commit, a tag and change the manifest.

We decide the version with the patch|minor|major command.

Maybe, we can use a mix of pbr and bumpversion to change the manifest version based in (something):
 - Maybe a standard of commit message
 - Maybe a tag for each module
 - Maybe a mix of: auto-increase just patch sections but for minor and major use manually.

I don't know what is the better solution for 2 worlds.

>>> between plain dictionaries and model instances, they are merely design
>>> issues that can be fixed.
>>>
>>> One scenario where, historically, there has been a significant
>>> performance penalty is basic validation. Passing a dict to validate()
>>> is efficient because both conversion and validation are performed at once:
>>>
>>> validate(MyModel, inputdict)
>>>
>>> Doing the same thing using an instance involves importing first and
>>> validating after:
>>>
>>> MyModel(inputdict).validate()
>>>
>>> Since validation also has the conversion step baked in, the whole import
>>> process is run twice for no reason.
>>>
>>> This is addressed in PR #374
>>> <https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/374>, which improves the
>>> performance of the above scenario by ~40%. The functional variant is now
>>> only about 3% faster than creating and validating instances.
>>>
>> This is good news.
>>
>> Memory consumption is another consideration when using instances. Python
>> classes are built on top of dict's, which all must have considerations on
>> top for growing memory structures, and all must have unique allocations.
>>
>> Class instances, by nature, require more resources.
>>
>> Alas, Python isn't the fastest language out there.
>>

See data format examples at http://python-crfsuite.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pycrfsuite.html#pycrfsuite.ItemSequence - the format you're using is just a shortcut:

> {“string_key”: “string_value”, ...} dict; that’s the same as {“string_key=string_value”: 1.0, ...}

All features have float values, both in crfsuite and python-crfsuite; `{"w[-1]": "a"}` is just a shortcut for `{"w[-1]=a": 1.0}`, and in crfsuite example `=` is just a convention. 1.0 is an assumed value; in crfsuite you override it by putting after `:`, in python-crfsuite you can override it by using another feature format (`{"string_key": float_value}`). 

So if you want such disjunctive features, pass `{"w[1...3]=eats": 1.0}` instead of `{"w[1...3]": "eats"}`. If you don't need weights, you can also use a list feature format.

v0.9.4 is officially released! See [PyPi](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/confluent-kafka/0.9.4).

And it has get_watermark_offsets()!

If you are actively consuming the partitions you should use cached=True and then the cost of calling it once per partition is miniscule (for the non-cached variant, not so much).
The high-watermark is updated on each Fetch response for the given partition, which should be every <= fetch.wait.max.ms (def 100ms).

The returned tuple is the high and low watermark offsets: the highest offset available in the partition (latest message) and the lowest offset available in the partition (the oldest message).

The high watermark is updated frequently as noted above, but the low watermark is updated every statistics.interval.ms - and if you don't need the low watermark then leave statistics.interval.ms set to 0 to avoid the extra requests.

It is not possible to put messages back on the consumer queue, but you can control what messages are to be committed by disable enable.auto.offset.store and explicitly calling store_offsets() when you want to increase the high watermark of messages processed.
This ensures that the auto committer will only commit messages that you have processed.

More info:
https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/issues/300#issuecomment-358416432

Ever since 1.0 colorschemes consist of three parts (and this is described in the documentation): colorschemes/(name).json, colorschemes/(ext)/__main__.json and colorschemes/(ext)/(name).json, of which any file may be missing (but either the first or the last must be present). These files are merged with each other to form the resulting colorscheme.

So missing wm/default.json file is not the issue. The fact that without color definitions segment is not going to work is not either. Since you have not setup logging then disappearing error is neither (maybe it is an issue that this error is displayed *at all* **in the bar**). This happens in `OpenAPIConverter.field2property`.

There is currently no way to extend this method.

As a workaround, you could make your own `OpenAPIConverter` child class and override `field2property`, then override `MarshmallowPlugin` to use it. This implies a lot of duplication in `field2property`, so it is far from perfect.

A better solution would be to let the user specify `field2property` methods to map to field types, assuming that's even feasible.

We could keep a `FIELD_TO_PROPERTY` mapping:

```py

FIELD_TO_PROPERTY = {
    flask_marshmallow.fields.Hyperlinks: hyperlink_field2property,
}
```
Each methods in the mapping would return a dict.

Then in `OpenAPIConverter.field2property`

```py
        [...]
        elif isinstance(field, marshmallow.fields.Dict):
            [...]
        # Manage custom fields
        elif type(field) in FIELD_TO_PROPERTY:
            ret.update(FIELD_TO_PROPERTY[type(field)](field, use_refs=use_refs, dump=dump, name=name))
        [...]
```

Then, you'd "just" have to create `hyperlink_field2property` so that it returns what you expect.

By definition, `Function` fields can return anything. What would you expect apispec to do?

Yeah, this is a common gotcha. The reason is that `named_configs` behave like config updates and are evaluated **before** the configuration. They should have probably been called `named_updates` instead.
 
The arguments of a named config can only refer to updates passed from the commandline. So calling the first version with `ex.run(named_configs=['variant1'], config_updates={'a': 'oof'})` should work.

> You can specify that your code works with different versions of Lasagne fine with both setup.py and requirements.txt.

Let's say I've got Lasagne installed with `python setup.py develop`, and that other code that depends on Lasagne doesn't even have a `setup.py`, but is just part of an experiment specification (similar to a Pylearn2 config file). Should I have a `requirements.txt` for every config file to record the commit of Lasagne I had when writing it? Should my training script collect the different `requirements.txt` files and do something with it before running? It would seem more convenient to me to add some "minimum-version" check or version switch to the config files that need it. (Granted, I'm not sure that would be very often, but my crystal ball tells me it would be useful some time.)

> At install time you can install the required version if it's not there, at runtime you can just bail out.

You can also be compatible with multiple versions via a `if version < ...: do this; else: do that`. (Granted, that's only useful when working with an unreleased version that may introduce breaking changes. To react on non-breaking changes like additions to the library, it's easier to directly check if a particular class or function is defined.)

> I'm not someone who uses every new feature immediately.

Say what?! :) No, that's not what I was implying. It's enough that in our target audience, there will always be someone who wants the new feature we just added (say, some idea from a recent paper), so there will always be users that will use a version of Lasasgne between releases. And it wouldn't be bad to have `__version__` produce useful output for them, especially when they find bugs.

> Still a big -1 from me for any of this (IMO) overcomplicated versions stuff.

Yes, I've sensed that :)


Soo... I don't feel that strongly about pep440-style versions, I just thought they'd be very nice to have. If the majority is against it, we can just have a fixed `__version__ = 'something'` line in `__init__.py` that's set to a version number for every release, and to `'bleeding-edge'` for every commit that's not a release. Basically, that's saying: "Either use one of the releases, or use the latest git commit and don't fall behind".

>  The dictionary just maps a layer l_inp to be iterated over to a cell input.

One concern I have about this is that currently, dictionaries are used in Lasagne to:
- map names to layers, e.g. `{ 'foo': layer_foo }`
- map layers to Theano expressions, e.g. `{ layer_foo: x }`

These two cases are easily distinguished visually because the former will typically have string keys, and the latter will have layer references as keys.

If I understand correctly, the `RecurrentContainerLayer` would take a dictionary mapping `Layer` instances to other `Layer` instances, which is not easily visually distinguishable from the 2nd case. As a seasoned Lasagne user it would definitely confuse me if I saw it for the first time. Maybe there is some way we can make this usage more 'distinct'? This is not a showstopper of course, I'm just thinking about how we can make the API as intuitive as possible.

But keep in mind that the use case I mentioned is only one of many. All these methods have various arguments that you might want to tweak:
* `upload_fileobj` and `download_fileobj` have `ExtraArgs` (already mentioned) and `Config` for configuring how the network transfer is actually done. `Callback` is pretty interesting as well.
* `copy_object` has a ton of arguments for all kinds of things.
* `generate_presigned_url` has the pretty important `ExpiresIn` argument and it proxies `Params` to `get_object` which also has a ton of arguments.

So far I only needed to pass along the file type (after figuring it out with libmagic) but the expiration time of the URL will probably be the next thing I need to configure. Then I'd need to set `ResponseContentDisposition` dynamically depending on which client is asking for the file and in what context (sometimes the want to just display it in the browser, sometimes they need an attachment). And finally `Callback` might come in handy to update the client on the upload progress.

Generic core mechanisms sound good but they need to be carefully conceptualized and that takes time for all these use cases. So making these methods overridable was the quickest yet still backwards compatible way to fulfill my requirements.

> Originally, we had written our own tiny implementation of NPM which would run in the browser (calling the official NPM API endpoint, locating tarballs, unpacking them in the browser, and loading the files appropriately).

In fact, nowadays Carbide's "Current Window" mode uses the same SystemJS loader for both the editor itself and the code which gets executed on it. This means that it'll technically be able to self-host, hot-reload, and instrument various parts of itself. That's kinda neat.

I haven't been able to get SystemJS/jspm to dynamically import an npm module from within the browser (e.g. in a Jupyter notebook) yet, but if that's possible like they're implying it is, then we could potentially expose a `module` property and the value would be an npm module name!

Regarding callbacks, ideally you would define a callback in the native language (e.g. Python) so that the kernel can respond to events in React. I really have no idea how to implement that but I think [jupyter-react](https://github.com/timbr-io/jupyter-react) may have figured it out. jupyter-react involves:

1. A [notebook extension](https://github.com/timbr-io/jupyter-react-example/blob/master/js/src/index.js) for initializing/registering React component(s) on the front-end
2. [Python classes](https://github.com/timbr-io/jupyter-react-example/blob/master/example/thing.py) for each React component to handle comm with its component on the front-end
3. [webpack](https://github.com/timbr-io/jupyter-react-example/blob/master/js/webpack.config.js) for building the JS

So it's a lot of work to get just render some data in a React component. However, I imagine that:

1. `display_id` could be used to send comm messages from kernel to front-end
2. The display method could handle comm message from front-end to kernel
3. _Maybe_ SystemJS could allow React components on npm to used directly in the browser

> the relationship between the memcpy size and whether it will cause either

> memcpy to report that it has the opportunity to segfault (true!) and halt
> emulation. This could theoretically be fixed by adding a maximum to the
> buffer sizes based on the memory mapping, but that would prevent angr from
> being used to find segfaults caused by memcpy...

For those who are looking at this and want a solution for the moment, I've found that using

```python
on_message('COMMAND_ACK')
```

and 

```python
on_message('STATUSTEXT')
```

work relatively well for detecting failure. However, as mentioned in the best practices guide, I think checking state often and explicitly, as well as polling on command success is the most reliable. That said, being able to see when there is a problem early is nice. It's also nice to be able to log the cause of failure (I send all output from both listeners to logs). 

COMMAND_ACK is the MAVLink message for acknowledging the success or failure of a command, while STATUSTEXT is the MAVLink message for delivering plain text (as in, the output you see in MAVProxy). For me, on COMMAND_ACK, if the command is an arm or takeoff command and has a non-zero result (i.e. failure), I send the LAND command to immediately abort. STATUSTEXT also reports a severity which you can possibly act on. You can read about it under the section "MAV_SEVERITY" [in the specification here](http://mavlink.org/messages/common).

I don't think we can hope to have a ```setUp``` which looks just like ```from ganga import *``` I think we'll still need to have some logic to do things like re-generate repositories when the location changes and things like this.
It would be nice to see more of the code from ```GangaUnitTest``` put into simple helper functions.

I think from the perspective of tests we need:
 0. Load the Config (if required)
 1. Bootstrap ganga (still required for the moment from the Config system).
 2. Initialize the Registries
 3. Start any Ganga services demanded by the Config. (Monitoring, Tasks, Queues....)

At the end of each test (as in shutDownClass) we at least need to:
 1. Shutdown Ganga services currently running
 2. Shutdown the Registries (This takes care of the lockfile monitoring as well)
 3. Clear the Registry and on-disk elements we've modified.

TODO (feel free to correct me but I think these items need addressing, although probably not in order):

 1. Move delattr of Ganga.GPI Objects into shutdown methods to make sure the GPI after a shutdown is really clean. This may require care with the credentials system as it is.
 2. Separate tearDown from tearDownClass functions which can be tricky but allows us to only shutdown the registries once per test which is a bit more sane than the current system.
 3. Take common code from GangaUnitTest and bootstrap and put them into methods which are common to both and integrate these into 'ganga'.
 4. Separate loading of the config/plugins from starting of the registries and populating of the GPI objects.
 5. Possible cleanup of ```ganga/__init__.py``` after everything is working so we have a single function to load the config, then call the functions to load the GPI objects and then call the function to start the services.

Yes, my intended use case is actually to patch an existing one.

In fact, by configuring it like this:
```
collections:
   local_fallback:
       sequence:
            - name: local
               index_paths: ./collections/local/indexes
               archive_paths: ./collections/local/archives

             - name: live
                index_paths: $live

recorder:
    source_coll: local_fallback
    source_filter: live
```

A call to `/local/record/http://www.example.com` would add the page to the `local` collection if not already exist.

Having an `/patch` endpoint or `skip_duplicate: True` option in the recorder config would indeed be great. It's more intuitive and user-friendly. (I took me some time to figure out how to lay out a fallback sequence with filter to achieve the same). Also it would avoid the complexity of maintaining group of patches to the master collection (and replay with aggregate collections).

There is an implementation difficulty, however. By `/patch` , the end user would expect two consecutive `/patch` call to the same URL should only generate a single WARC record. However, the current proposed algorithm could not know it is a duplicate until the next reindexing. Using deduplication by Redis server (I see it is to be implemented) would be an alternative, but I think is quite undesirable having to maintain another Redis server. Or is there any other way? Maybe having an update-index-upon-warc-write? Would there be too much performance overhead?  

By `source_filter: live`,  I think it is more clear to the user that consecutive call to same URL can still generate multiple record. 

In short,

> Is this the use case that you're imagining? 
Yes. 

> Do you anticipate wanting to do this for multiple collection?
Not for my own usage, but I could imagine this would be useful for other users/developers

> If so, the /patch endpoint may seem useful.
Agree but it need some thought on how to properly implement `/patch`

> Do you want to have the patched content be separate from the original collection, as would happen here, or added to that collection?
Personally I would prefer to add it to the original collection, with the config setup above.

Let's come back to the basics ...

The main idea of this is to display two numbers over the icons. The first number is the 'hosts' count and the second is the 'hosts in problem' count. the icon is colored depending upon an host warning/critical threshold.

When everything is ok, we get this kind of view:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2932687/17856057/ffde2670-687b-11e6-923f-c9788be0e830.png)

When some problems exist, we get this: 
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2932687/17858796/78e6c210-6887-11e6-9b9e-d22cc4e8750e.png)

Hosts problems are over the critical threshold (eg. red)
Services problems are below the warning threshold (eg. green)
Global problems are between the warning and critical threshold (eg. orange)

Note that currently impacts are not managed ...

On my side, I do not have any other idea for creating a view that will allow to display all those information on a more suitable manner ... any ideas to improve are welcome :wink:

Since you're using the login view from django-two-factor-auth Axes isn't patching your views to actually bounce the logins when the failures happen. You do have the access logs populating because Axes receives the signals from login which django-two-factor-auth apparently sends, but uses custom login views.

The problems are here:

https://github.com/jazzband/django-axes/blob/master/axes/apps.py#L15
https://github.com/jazzband/django-axes/blob/master/axes/decorators.py#L28

As you can see Axes decorates the logins and rejects the logins in the custom decorators, which are not applied to your django-two-factor-auth.

One possibility would be to add the decoration step to your application so that the django-two-factor-auth views are decorated as well. This could be placed, for example, in your `urls.py` if you import the django-two-factor-auth login URLs by hand and just wrap in the decorators.

Custom authentication and authorization packages always require a bit of extra work and study to patch together if they are not well integrated already :)

Let me quote the spec on `MGMT_ACTIVE_SET.req` because it has similar phrasing with respect to the 'may'
p5-56 L32
> The Commissioner and Thread devices may provide a new Active Operational Dataset to the Leader by sending a MGMT_ACTIVE_SET.req.

This part has, similarly to MGMT_ACTIVE_GET.req, a 'may' in the text (note this is not a 'MAY'). However, in section 8.7.4.2 the spec says:
> A Thread device that has a newer Active Operational Dataset, as determined by the Active Timestamp included in Thread Network Data, MUST provide its newer Active Operational Dataset to the Leader by sending a MGMT_ACTIVE_SET.req to the Leader. 

This text has a clear 'MUST'. In my opinion the 'may' more means _when it is required_ rather than _it is up to the device to decide if it sends or doesn't send this message_.

With respect to `MGMT_ACTIVE_SET.req` p8-20 L37 of the spec says:
> If the Parent advertises an Active Timestamp that is different that the Thread device’s local Active Timestamp, the Thread device MUST retrieve and install the Active Operational Dataset from its Parent.

How else would it retrieve the dataset than by using `MGMT_ACTIVE_SET.req`

That being said. It is not because the functionality exists it has to be made available through the API. 
This brings me back to my first post. In my opinion the `otSendMgmtActiveGet` is something that should be available through the API for all devices, not excluding MTDs. On the other hand I don't think `otSendMgmtActiveSet` should be available through the API, because a stack event will trigger a `MGMT_ACTIVE_SET.req`, while any  other (user) attempt of changing the active dataset should pass through the commissioner.

You can reinstall TFLearn, I set allow_empty=True by default.

I see, you data are very large, so you probably can't fit them in your RAM memory. The best way for you is to use HDF5 to handle large datasets, it is compatible with TFLearn. Basically, it will load your data from your disk directly, instead of loading your data into RAM memory, so you can handle GBs of data without problem.
You just need to save your data into h5 format (https://www.hdfgroup.org/HDF5/, http://www.h5py.org/).
One basic example that show hdf5 compatibility with TFLearn: https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/examples/basics/use_hdf5.py (It is very easy, because you can just fit(X,Y) and train the whole dataset).

I was the one who opened the issue originally. I did so because when I saw the `ppaged()` method I noticed that it had a hardcoded `less` with a set of options that truncate long lines. It seemed a bit unfriendly to have a default implementation which truncated long lines with no way for the end user to change it.

On *nix-ish systems, there is a long standing tradition that the user can set their desired pager and options using the `PAGER` environment variable. So I thought this might be a more useful default.

Any developer of `cmd2` based applications can create their own implementation of `ppaged()`, or create a new method to buffer output to the screen. If the developer wants to tightly control the options to the pager to get the desired line wrapping behavior, let them do it.

In this case we need to decide whether we want to be developer friendly by default, or user friendly by default. If we chose developer friendly, then we wouldn't add a way for the user to override the pager. We would choose a sensible default behavior for `ppaged()` and if developers want different, they can write their own implementation.

If we chose user friendly, then we would do something like @tleonhardt has implemented here, and if developers want it differently, they can hide the setting and make their own implementation of `ppaged()`.

I have no preference either way, but I don't think truncating long lines of output is a good default behavior.

* Add a version number to each bootstrap script that must be incremented when new OS dependencies are added.
* Write a function in `certbot-auto` that returns the current dependency version string for the OS.
* Add a `--os-version` flag to `certbot-auto` that has it print this version string and exit. After downloading a new `certbot-auto` but before replacing the currently running script, execute something like the following bashy pseudocode:
```
MY_OS_VERSION=$(GetOSVersion)
NEW_OS_VERSION=$("$TEMP_DIR/letsencrypt-auto.permission-clone" --os-version)
if $MY_OS_VERSION != $NEW_OS_VERSION; then
  if $ASSUME_YES = 1 || (test -t 0 && test -t 1); then
    rm -rf "$VENV_PATH"
    $SUDO mv -f "$TEMP_DIR/letsencrypt-auto.permission-clone" "$0"
  else
    echo Skipping upgrade because new OS dependencies must be installed and we want your approval.
  fi
else
  $SUDO mv -f "$TEMP_DIR/letsencrypt-auto.permission-clone" "$0"
fi
```
I kind of hate the terminal checks. Alternatively, we could prompt the user asking if they want to upgrade knowing they must install additional OS dependencies, but we'd also have to implement a timeout and this approach is significantly more work for minimal gain. If anyone has any ideas here they like better, I'd love to hear them.

We also currently treat the usage of `renew` on the command line the same as `--non-interactive`, but I'm going to remove that. Otherwise, people who throw `certbot-auto renew` in a cron job without `--no-self-upgrade` just automatically get whatever OS dependencies we want to install on their system. `certbot-auto` not asking for permission to do these things pissed people off before so let's not do it again.

```
from glob import glob

import numpy as np

import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib import colors

from pymchelper.io import fromfilelist
from pymchelper.detector import ErrorEstimate


filelist = glob('1/temp/*.bdo')
print(filelist)
det = fromfilelist(filelist, error=ErrorEstimate.stddev, nan=False)

# pymchelper could guess from you which axes holds the data for 2d plot (in this case Y and Z)
first_mesh_axis = det.plot_axis(0)
second_mesh_axis = det.plot_axis(1)

# you can also select the axis manually
# swapping Y and Z would require matplotlib hacking
first_mesh_axis = det.y
second_mesh_axis = det.z

# set labels
plt.xlabel(first_mesh_axis.name + " [" + first_mesh_axis.unit + "]")
plt.ylabel(second_mesh_axis.name + " [" + second_mesh_axis.unit + "]")

# matplotlib needs 2D arrays xlist and ylist for heatmap plotting
# we use XX_mesh_axis.data 1D arrays to generate them
xlist, ylist = np.meshgrid(first_mesh_axis.data, second_mesh_axis.data)

# in pymchelper we have 3D and 1D representation of scored quantity
# here we do reshaping of 1D representation to get 2D array for heatmap plotting
shape_tuple = (second_mesh_axis.n, first_mesh_axis.n)
zlist = det.data_raw.reshape(shape_tuple)
print(zlist.shape)

# logarithmic norm for dose wash, you can change it to linear one
norm = colors.LogNorm(vmin=zlist[zlist > 0].min(), vmax=zlist.max())

# plot heatmap
plt.pcolormesh(xlist, ylist, zlist, norm=norm)

# set colorbar
cbar = plt.colorbar()
cbar.set_label(det.name + " [" + det.unit + "]", rotation=270, verticalalignment='bottom')

plt.savefig("plot.png")
plt.close()
```

    return sense2vec.load(model_path)  # load vectors here


def get_s2v(span):
    model = load_model(span.doc._.s2v_model)
    return  # compute the result here


class Sense2VecComponent(object):
    def __init__(self, model_path):
        self.model = model_path
        load_model(model_path)  # preload model

    def __call__(self, doc):
        doc._.s2v_model = self.model
        # etc.
        return doc
```

The above would then also allow usage like:

```python
nlp1 = spacy.load('en')
nlp2 = spacy.load('de')

s2v1 = Sense2VecComponent('./en_vectors')
s2v2 = Sense2VecComponent('./de_vectors')
```

> Why do you say the trace back is not important for sos actions?

I should say that `RuntimeError` (or more specifically in the case of `ret != 0`, this very specific type of error) in actions should not report trace. Because it is wordy and redundant -- all the info, and more, are also raised in the actual error message. For example currently:

```
ERROR: [sra ['SRR_1.fastq']]: 
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
RuntimeError                              Traceback (most recent call last)
script_-7594117775864177549 in <module>
      run(r"""fastq-dump -I --split-files {os.path.basename(data)}
----> """)

RuntimeError: Failed to execute commmand "/bin/bash -ev /tmp/tmpswhu8nto.sh" (ret=2, workdir=/home/gaow/tmp/28-Apr-2018, script now in /home/gaow/tmp/28-Apr-2018/.sos/sra_0_672e66db.sh, err=/tmp/test.err)
```

The trace info is not informative because it is not useful to debug or reproduce. Instead one should really focus on the mentioned temp script and the `err` file to find out what is going on. Because the error is not about workflow specification, it is about `fastq-dump` went wrong. Having the trace there is distracting. However it is otherwise useful, for example for any other error not in the `subprocess` call, eg, if I mess with the script I get trace info before the run:

```
ERROR: Failed to execute script: [Errno 21] Is a directory: '/tmp'
ERROR: [sra ['SRR_1.fastq']]: 
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
IsADirectoryError                         Traceback (most recent call last)
script_2345474589931989838 in <module>
      run(r"""fastq-dump -I --split-files {os.path.basename(data)}
----> """, stderr='/tmp')

IsADirectoryError: 21

```

that is, I specified `stderr='/tmp'` which is a folder, and it is my bad SoS code. Then it makes to raise trace error (though it is not super helpful in this case..). 

My point is, that we should have a clear distinction of action error -- which is typically outside of scope of SoS, and coding / syntax error -- which reports trace and ask users to fix. To me also I find trace usually means a bug in code, so displaying a trace error when in fact there is no error in the code per se is also distracting. That's also why I got the complaint that "I cannot understand the error message" even when we are perfectly clear that they should run some command or look at some log file to reproduce!

Another small point: our task tree introspection APIs aren't fully fleshed out, but the old concept was to represent it as a tree of tasks, with nurseries an invisible details. And this was fine; it didn't really make sense for a task to have more than one nursery in it, because once it had one nursery it had to park in `__aexit__` ("parenting is a full-time job"). But if we're changing things so that parenting isn't a full time job, and the code inside the nursery block is more-or-less like a little inline child task, then there's no particular reason that nested nurseries can't happen, so our introspection API should reflect that reality. Plus we were already tracking task→parent nursery→parent task links anyway, just hiding it in the introspection API, and for #284 I had to start tracking task→child nursery links, so we might as well provide this information.

So tasks should have `parent_nursery` and `child_nurseries` attributes, and nurseries should `parent_task` and `child_tasks` attributes.

exposing the raw OS capabilities, and "wait for arbitrary file descriptor"
is a thing that Unix supports and Windows doesn't. (And even on Unix there
are some funny limitations. Most notably: files on disk will claim to
always be readable/writable.) I wish it were otherwise, but that's
something we'd have to take up with Microsoft about 20 years ago.

Iterating on bytes wastes a lot of CPU. I wouldn't want to do that, at least not as default.

However, what I think would have merit is to add an async iterator to `ReceiveStream` which simply periodically calls `receive_some` (with some buffer size) and yields that. That's a great point, @benclifford. Nothing dynamic happens in the config constructors-- that is controlled by the DFK with the `start` method. But the correct way to do this is probably to instantiate a new object with the same calling arguments as the original. @ZhuozhaoLi you can search through the code for the `signature` module to see places where we do similar things.

@joezuntz For this case we need to set [retries](http://parsl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/userguide/exceptions.html?highlight=retries#retries) > 1, so that jobs which fail from workers hitting walltime will be re-launched. Whenever there is more work than workers, parsl will try to get more compute from the site. I'd recommend you take a quick look at [ execution](http://parsl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/userguide/execution.html#elasticity). Setting `parallelism == 1`, makes sense for long running jobs like yours.

Hi @Sturgelose,

The client.CoreV1Api() object use dependencies injection to get its ApiClient.
We can use this to create our own ApiClient, then inject it.

To create our own K8sApiClient, we are using inheritance of the ApiClient, then we override some functions.

Here comes the patched K8sApiClient:
```
class K8sApiClient(client.ApiClient):
    def call_api(self, *args, async=None, **kwargs):
        return super().call_api(*args, async=False, **kwargs)

    def __init__(self, configuration=None, header_name=None, header_value=None, cookie=None):
        if configuration is None:
            configuration = Configuration()
        self.configuration = configuration

#        self.pool = ThreadPool()
        self.rest_client = RESTClientObject(configuration)
        self.default_headers = {}
        if header_name is not None:
            self.default_headers[header_name] = header_value
        self.cookie = cookie
        # Set default User-Agent.
        self.user_agent = 'Swagger-Codegen/4.0.0/python'

    def __del__(self):
        pass
```

Then how to use it:
```
        config = client.Configuration()
        api_client = K8sApiClient(configuration=config)
        coreapi = client.CoreV1Api(api_client)
        batchapi = client.BatchV1Api(api_client)
```

It's just some copy/paste from a project, it's not tested code. But you should have everything to disable this ThreadPool issue.

This is expected. The `CurrentUser` object is read-only (this comes from the GitLab API: https://docs.gitlab.com/ce/api/users.html#user). To modify a user you need to create a `User` object. For example in your context:

```python
# lazy=False avoids a new API call
editable_user = session.users.get(session.user.id, lazy=False)
editable_user.bio = 'test1'
editable_user.save()
```

Hi @doru1004 ,

If auto_pad = 'VALID' that mean not auto_padding, in this case if pads is specified then use the value in `pads` attribute, otherwise no padding is applied.

Here is the logic, 
    If auto_pad = SAME_LOWER or SAME_UPPER, then ignore `pads` attribute.
    If auto_pad = VALID, then check `pads` attribute. If pads attribute isn't empty, then use it. Otherwise no padding.

Update about geometry and polygons: 

In newly released `sentinelhub` version `2.1.0` we added `CustomUrlParam.GEOMETRY` which can be used to specify geometry. E.g.:

```Python
wms_request = WmsRequest( ... , 
    custom_url_params={
        CustomUrlParam.GEOMETRY: "POLYGON((1292344.0 5205055.5, 1301479.5 5195920.0, 1310615.0 5205055.5, 1301479.5 5214191.0, 1292344.0 5205055.5))"
    }
)
```

As you can see geometry has to be specified as a string in wkt format (the same way as when calling Sentinel Hub services directly). Also coordinates have to be in the same CRS as the `BBox`.


#### Improvement for the future:
We could add wrapper classes for geometrical elements (e.g. `class Polygon`). Then geometry could be specified like this:

`CustomUrlParam.GEOMETRY: Polygon(...)` 

where `Polygon` would handle geometry parsing and CRS transformations.

That would take more time to implement therefore I am closing this issue for now.

## tl;dr

On SERVER, use
```
jupyter notebook --no-browser \
  --NotebookApp.allow_origin='https://colab.research.google.com' \
  --port=8888 \
  --NotebookApp.token='' --NotebookApp.password='' \
  --NotebookApp.disable_check_xsrf=True
```

On LAPTOP use
```
ssh ME@SERVER -L 8888:localhost:8888 -N
```

Connect to colab from LAPTOP and use Local runtime on port `8888` and it works.

----

## Long version

I used to have the exact same error:

On SERVER
```
jupyter notebook --no-browser \
  --NotebookApp.allow_origin='https://colab.research.google.com' \
  --port=8888 \
  --NotebookApp.token='' --NotebookApp.password=''
```
(I disabled all security to be sure it was not a problem).

On LAPTOP
```
ssh -F/dev/null ME@SERVER -L 9999:localhost:8888  -N
```
(I disabled the usual ssh configuration with `-F/dev/null` just to be sure, you may not need it)

On LAPTOP I can connect to `http://localhost:9999/` and I get the jupyter interface.
From colab, trying to connect to the Local runtime on port `9999` gives "Unable to connect" and the following message on SERVER:
```
[E 18:13:45.474 NotebookApp] Uncaught error when proxying request
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/jupyter_http_over_ws/handlers.py", line 151, in on_message
        response.rethrow()
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/tornado/httpclient.py", line 652, in rethrow
        raise self.error
    ConnectionRefusedError: [Errno 111] Connection refused
```

However, setting the port-forward to the same port (`8888` rather than `9999`) gave me the following error on SERVER:
```
[W 18:13:24.141 NotebookApp] 403 POST /api/sessions (::1): '_xsrf' argument missing from POST
[W 18:13:24.141 NotebookApp] '_xsrf' argument missing from POST
[W 18:13:24.142 NotebookApp] 403 POST /api/sessions (::1) 2.40ms referer=None
```

Which led me to add the ` --NotebookApp.disable_check_xsrf=True` option when running the jupyter notebook.

So it seems related to a port error. I have now idea why.

Also, I would welcome tips on how to allow connection without removing all security (no token, no password, etc.).
